Individual interviewees were also asked whether the community was aware of the carbon credit agreement (Table 3). Most respondents indicated that the community was aware, but the percentage of positive responses varied across interviewee roles. Interestingly, the NGO represenatatives (a groups that also included carbon enterprise representatives), overwhelmingly (~95%) suggested the community was informed, but the only 70% of DFOs thought the communities were informed. However, none of these differences were significant.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  select(
    interviewee_role,
    empl = economic_transformation_community_awareness_carbon_credit_agreement
  ) |>
  mutate(
    interviewee_role = case_match(
      as.character(interviewee_role),
      "cfmg_member"      ~ "Community member",
      "cfmg_exec_member" ~ "CFMG executive member",
      "trad_leader"      ~ "Traditional leader",
      "dfo"              ~ "District Forestry Officer",
      "NGO_rep"          ~ "NGO representative"
    ),
    interviewee_role = factor(interviewee_role, levels = c(
      "Community member", "CFMG executive member", "Traditional leader",
      "District Forestry Officer", "NGO representative"
    )),
    empl = case_match(empl,
                      "yes" ~ 1,
                      "no"  ~ 0,
                      .default = NA)
  ) |>
  group_by(interviewee_role) |>
  summarise(
    n         = n(),
    Awareness = mean(empl, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups   = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(interviewee_role) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    interviewee_role = "Role",
    n                = "n",
    Awareness        = "Aware of carbon credit agreement"
  ) |>
  fmt_percent(
    columns  = Awareness,
    decimals = 0
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Community awareness of the carbon credit agreement, as reported by individual interviewees",
    subtitle = "Values represent the % of positive responses. n = the total number of interview responses."
  ) |>
  opt_align_table_header(align = "left") |>
  cols_align(align = "left",   columns = interviewee_role) |>
  cols_align(align = "center", columns = c(n, Awareness)) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(align = "center"),
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = interviewee_role)
  ) |>
  sub_missing()

NoteCarbon finance awareness – Individual interviewees – Model methods





To examine differences among individual interviewee roles in whether they reported community awareness of the carbon credit agreement, we employed a generalised linear mixed model (GLMM) with a binomial response and random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We included interviewee role, interviewee gender and interviewee age as factors. To examine pairwise differences among interviewee roles, we employed Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data.ca.ind <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    empl = case_match(
      economic_transformation_community_awareness_carbon_credit_agreement,
      "yes" ~ 1,
      "no"  ~ 0,
      .default = NA
    ),
    interviewee_gender = demo_information_interviewee_gender,
    interviewee_age    = demo_information_interviewee_age
  ) |>
  drop_na(empl) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M12 <- glmer(empl ~ interviewee_role + interviewee_gender + interviewee_age +
              (1 | province) + (1 | province_cfmg),
            data = test.data.ca.ind, family = binomial)

emm.M12      <- emmeans(M12, specs = ~ interviewee_role, type = "response")
pairwise.M12 <- pairs(emm.M12, adjust = "Tukey")

Carbon trader support
The types of CFM activities supported by the carbon trader are shown in Figure 10. As can be seen support focused on aspects of forest management or agricultural extension. Other potential elements of support, such as NTFPs, infrastructure development or market access, featured substantially less.


Code
prefix_ts <- "economic_transformation_cfm_activities_supported_by_carbon_trader_"

ts_labels <- c(
  "patrolling_and_monitoring"                        = "Patrolling",
  "fire_management"                                  = "Fire management",
  "biodiversity_conservation"                        = "Biodiversity",
  "agroforestry"                                     = "Agroforestry",
  "climate_smart_farming"                            = "Climate-smart farming",
  "non_timber_forest_products_ntfps"                 = "NTFPs",
  "eco_tourism"                                      = "Eco-tourism",
  "tree_planting"                                    = "Tree planting",
  "training_and_capacity_building"                   = "Training",
  "carbon_stock_tracking"                            = "Carbon tracking",
  "market"                                           = "Market access",
  "alternative_livelihood_activities"                = "Livelihoods",
  "infractrure"                                      = "Infrastructure",
  "social_gatherings"                                = "Social gatherings",
  "employment"                                       = "Employment",
  "community_sensitization_awareness_engangement"    = "Sensitisation"
)

full_ts_vars <- paste0(prefix_ts, names(ts_labels))

ts_freq <- cfmg |>
  select(all_of(full_ts_vars)) |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(. == 1, na.rm = TRUE))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "freq") |>
  mutate(word = ts_labels[sub(prefix_ts, "", variable, fixed = TRUE)]) |>
  filter(freq > 0) |>
  select(word, freq)

set.seed(42)
ggplot(ts_freq, aes(label = word, size = freq, colour = freq)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud(eccentricity = 0.65, seed = 42) +
  scale_size(range = c(3, 18)) +
  scale_colour_gradient(low = "#6ABFB8", high = "#1A6B63") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0))








Figure 10: Types of CFM activities supported by the carbon trader.







Other income earning opportunities
FGD groups also reported whether CFM had generated income earning opportunities through specific interventions, including market linkages, alternative livelihood interventions, conservation revenue, forest product processing and ecotourism (Figure 11). Although some community FGDs indicated improvement in income earning opportunities under some categories, especially conservation revenue and forest product processing, most indicated that CFM had not generated new income earning opportunities in these areas.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type %in% c("cfmg_female", "cfmg_male", "cfmg_youth")) |>
  mutate(interview_type = case_match(
    interview_type,
    "cfmg_female" ~ "Female",
    "cfmg_male"   ~ "Male",
    "cfmg_youth"  ~ "Youth"
  )) |>
  select(
    interview_type,
    "Ecotourism income"         = economic_transformation_economic_benefits_from_cfm_income_from_ecotourism_e_g_guided_tours_camping_fees,
    "Forest product processing" = economic_transformation_economic_benefits_from_cfm_income_from_forest_product_processing_e_g_honey_bamboo,
    "Conservation revenue"      = economic_transformation_economic_benefits_from_cfm_revenue_from_forest_conservation_programs_e_g_carbon_credits,
    "Alternative livelihoods"   = `economic_transformation_economic_benefits_from_cfm_alternative-livelihoods_local_business_development_e_g_forest_based_crafts_small_scale_industries`,
    "Market linkages"           = economic_transformation_economic_benefits_from_cfm_market_linkages_and_trade_opportunities_for_forest_products
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(-interview_type, names_to = "income_source", values_to = "value") |>
  filter(!is.na(value)) |>
  mutate(
    response = case_match(as.character(value), "1" ~ "Yes", "0" ~ "No"),
    response = factor(response, levels = c("No", "Yes")),
    income_source = factor(income_source, levels = c(
      "Ecotourism income", "Forest product processing", "Conservation revenue",
      "Alternative livelihoods", "Market linkages"
    )),
    interview_type = factor(interview_type, levels = c("Female", "Male", "Youth"))
  ) |>
  count(interview_type, income_source, response) |>
  mutate(n_signed = if_else(response == "No", -n, n)) |>
  (\(d) ggplot(d, aes(y = income_source, x = n_signed, fill = response)) +
    geom_blank(data = tibble(
      interview_type = factor(
        c("Female", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Youth", "Youth"),
        levels = c("Female", "Male", "Youth")
      ),
      income_source = factor(
        rep("Ecotourism income", 6),
        levels = c("Ecotourism income", "Forest product processing",
                   "Conservation revenue", "Alternative livelihoods", "Market linkages")
      ),
      n_signed = c(-60, 0, -60, 10, -60, 10),
      response = factor(c("No", "Yes", "No", "Yes", "No", "Yes"), levels = c("No", "Yes"))
    )) +
    geom_col(width = 0.65, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0, colour = "white", linewidth = 1.2) +
    geom_text(
      aes(label  = n,
          hjust  = if_else(response == "No", -0.4, -0.4),
          colour = response),
      size = 3, fontface = "bold"
    ) +
    scale_colour_manual(
      values = c("No" = "white", "Yes" = "grey25"),
      guide  = "none"
    ) +
    scale_x_continuous(
      labels = ~abs(.x),
      breaks = seq(-60, 10, by = 20),
      expand = expansion(add = 0),
      name   = "Number of FGDs"
    ) +
    coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
    scale_y_discrete(expand = expansion(add = 0.5)) +
    scale_fill_manual(
      values = c("No" = "#E07B54", "Yes" = "#2A9D8F"),
      guide  = guide_legend(reverse = FALSE)
    ) +
    facet_wrap(~ interview_type, scales = "free_x") +
    labs(y = NULL, fill = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 18) +
    theme(
      panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
      axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
      axis.text.y        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
      axis.text.x        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
      axis.title.x       = element_text(size = rel(1), colour = "grey40"),
      legend.position    = "bottom",
      legend.text        = element_text(size = rel(1)),
      strip.text         = element_text(size = rel(1.14), face = "bold"),
      plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
    )
  )()








Figure 11: Income earning opportunities generated by CFM, as reported by female, male, and youth FGDs. The length of the bars represents the number of FGDs reporting a benefit (Yes) or no benefit (No) for each income source.





However, community female, male, and youth FGD groups did identified other additional sources of income generated by CFM (Figure 12). Natural resource based value chains featured prominently among these, but interestingly community business was also mentioned.


Code
prefix_income <- "economic_transformatioNAdditional_sources_of_income_additional_income_sources_from_cfm_"

income_freq <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type %in% c("cfmg_female", "cfmg_male", "cfmg_youth")) |>
  select(starts_with(prefix_income)) |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(. == 1, na.rm = TRUE))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "freq") |>
  mutate(
    word = variable |>
      sub(prefix_income, "", x = _, fixed = TRUE) |>
      gsub("_", " ", x = _) |>
      str_to_title(),
    word = case_match(word,
      "Community Businesses And Enterprises" ~ "Community business",
      "Wildlife Based And Safari Income"  ~ "Wildlife based",
      .default = word
    )
  ) |>
  filter(
    freq > 0,
    !str_starts(word, "Rank"),
    !word %in% c("None", "Dont Know")
  ) |>
  select(word, freq)

set.seed(42)
ggplot(income_freq, aes(label = word, size = freq, colour = freq)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud(eccentricity = 0.65, seed = 42) +
  scale_size(range = c(3, 22)) +
  scale_colour_gradient(low = "#6ABFB8", high = "#1A6B63") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0))








Figure 12: Additional sources of income from CFM reported by female, male, and youth FGDs. Word size is proportional to the number of FGDs reporting each income source.





To better understand the importance of different income earning opportunities we asked the community female, male, and youth FGD groups to rank their top three additional sources of income from CFM (Figure 13). Interestingly, Among the 14 value chains identified, 12 were mentioned as 1st rank at least once. This immediately points to the diversity of forest products being harvested from CFs across the country, but also to the fact that for most CFs only one product features strongly as an addition source of income. Among these, mushrooms and honey are clearly the most frequently mentioned.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type %in% c("cfmg_female", "cfmg_male", "cfmg_youth")) |>
  select(matches("Additional_sources_of_income.*rank_")) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "rank_val") |>
  mutate(
    rank_val = as.numeric(rank_val),
    source   = sub(".*rank_", "", variable) |>
      gsub("_", " ", x = _) |>
      str_to_title(),
    source = case_match(source,
      "Wildlife Based And Safari Income"  ~ "Wildlife Based",
      "Community Businesses And Enterprises" ~ "Community Business",
      .default = source
    )
  ) |>
  filter(rank_val %in% c(1, 2, 3), source != "Carbon Credits") |>
  mutate(rank = factor(paste0("Rank ", rank_val), levels = c("Rank 3", "Rank 2", "Rank 1"))) |>
  count(source, rank) |>
  group_by(source) |>
  mutate(total = sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(y = reorder(source, total), x = n, fill = rank)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = n),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    size = 3.5, colour = "white", fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05)),
    name   = "Number of FGDs"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Rank 1" = "#264653",
      "Rank 2" = "#E9C46A",
      "Rank 3" = "#E07B54"
    ),
    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)
  ) +
  labs(y = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 18) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_line(colour = "grey85", linewidth = 0.4),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.title.x       = element_text(size = rel(1), colour = "grey40"),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    legend.text        = element_text(size = rel(1)),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )








Figure 13: Ranking of additional income sources from CFM by female, male, and youth FGDs. Bars show the number of FGDs assigning each rank. Only income sources receiving at least one top-three ranking are shown.





CFMG Executive FGDs were asked the estimated annual income generated through CFM (Figure 14). Unfortunately, there was a lot of inconsistency in the responses to this question. Most FGD groups did not include income from carbon credit sales, but some did. The figure below includes only those that did not include the carbon credit income to facilitate comparison.


Code

  select(
    province,

  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(income), income <= 250000) |>
  mutate(province = reorder(province, income, FUN = median)) |>
  ggplot(aes(y = province, x = income)) +

    width = 0.5, fill = "grey92", colour = "grey40",

  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = label_comma(),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.1)),
    name   = "Estimated annual income from CFM"
  ) +
  labs(y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 18) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_line(colour = "grey85", linewidth = 0.4),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.title.x       = element_text(size = rel(1), colour = "grey40"),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )

FGD groups also identified potential value-added opportunities from CFM resources (Figure 20). The overlap with the previous two questions is substantial, but it is interesting that the communities specifically identify honey processing and drying mushrooms, as well as timber processing. This suggests that the communities are aware that there value addition opportunities that they are currently not realising.

Opportunity costs

Precluded activities
The community female, male, and youth FGD groups identified economic activities that are precluded under CFM regulations (Figure 22). While many of these are self evident, the fact that NTFP harvesting is listed quite prominently is surprising. Whether this reflects a misunderstanding on the part of the communities or indeed that they are being told they cannot collect NTFPs is not clear.


Code

Other opportunity costs
FGD groups were asked whether CFM entails opportunity costs for the community (Figure 23). Interestingly, many of the FGDs did not recognise any opportunity costs. However, about a third of the FGDs did. The proportion of FGDs recognising opportunity costs was highest among community male FGDs and lowest among community female FGDs. However, none of the pairwise comparisons were significant.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type %in% c("cfmg_executive", "cfmg_female",
                                "cfmg_male", "cfmg_youth")) |>
  transmute(
    group = case_match(
      interview_type,
      "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
      "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
      "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
      "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
    ),
    response = case_when(
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm == "yes"          ~ "Yes",
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm == "no"           ~ "No",
      str_starts(economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm, "No_") ~ "No",
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm %in% c(
        "Community forest situated very far from community",
        "The Major livelihood for this community is fishing. They don't depend so much on the forest. They can still get materials for construction in the open areas"
      ) ~ "No",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(response) |>
  mutate(
    group    = factor(group, levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female", "Executive")),
    response = factor(response, levels = c("Yes", "No"))
  ) |>
  count(group, response) |>
  mutate(n_signed = if_else(response == "No", -n, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(y = group, x = n_signed, fill = response)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, colour = "white", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label  = n,
        hjust  = if_else(response == "No", -0.4, -0.4),
        colour = response),
    size = 4, fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("No" = "white", "Yes" = "grey25"),
                      guide = "none") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("No" = "#E07B54", "Yes" = "#2A9D8F"),
                    guide  = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = ~abs(.x),
                     expand = expansion(add = 3),
                     name   = "Number of FGDs") +
  scale_y_discrete(expand = expansion(add = 0.5)) +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
  labs(y = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 18) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text          = element_text(colour = "grey20"),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )








Figure 23: Whether CFM entails opportunity costs for the community, by FGD group.











NoteOpportunity costs – FGDs – Model methods





To examine differences among FGD types in whether they reported that CFM entails opportunity costs for the community, we employed a generalised linear mixed model (GLMM) with a binomial response and random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We used FGD type as the factor. We examined the significance of pairwise differences among FGD types using Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data.opp <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type %in% c("cfmg_executive", "cfmg_female",
                                "cfmg_male", "cfmg_youth")) |>
  transmute(
    province, cfmg_name,
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Executive", "Female", "Male", "Youth")
    ),
    empl = case_when(
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm == "yes"           ~ 1L,
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm == "no"            ~ 0L,
      str_starts(economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm, "No_") ~ 0L,
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm %in% c(
        "Community forest situated very far from community",
        "The Major livelihood for this community is fishing. They don't depend so much on the forest. They can still get materials for construction in the open areas"
      ) ~ 0L,
      TRUE ~ NA_integer_
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(empl) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M9 <- glmer(empl ~ interview_type + (1 | province) + (1 | province_cfmg),
            data = test.data.opp, family = binomial)

emm.M9      <- emmeans(M9, specs = ~ interview_type, type = "response")
pairwise.M9 <- pairs(emm.M9, adjust = "Tukey")

Individual interviewees were also asked whether CFM entails opportunity costs. Table 4 shows the percentage reporting this broken down by interviewee role. Interestingly, the community traditional leaders and community members recognise opportunity costs less of the time than other individual interviewee types, although these differences were not significant.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  select(
    interviewee_role,
    empl = economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm
  ) |>
  mutate(
    interviewee_role = case_match(
      as.character(interviewee_role),
      "cfmg_member"      ~ "Community member",
      "cfmg_exec_member" ~ "CFMG executive member",
      "trad_leader"      ~ "Traditional leader",
      "dfo"              ~ "District Forestry Officer",
      "NGO_rep"          ~ "NGO representative"
    ),
    interviewee_role = factor(interviewee_role, levels = c(
      "Community member", "CFMG executive member", "Traditional leader",
      "District Forestry Officer", "NGO representative"
    )),
    empl = case_when(
      empl == "yes"           ~ 1,
      empl == "no"            ~ 0,
      str_starts(empl, "No_") ~ 0,
      TRUE                    ~ NA_real_
    )
  ) |>
  group_by(interviewee_role) |>
  summarise(
    n             = n(),
    Opp_costs     = mean(empl, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups       = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(interviewee_role) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    interviewee_role = "Role",
    n                = "n",
    Opp_costs        = "Report opportunity costs"
  ) |>
  fmt_percent(
    columns  = Opp_costs,
    decimals = 0
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Opportunity costs of CFM, as reported by individual interviewees",
    subtitle = "Values represent the % of positive responses. n = the total number of interview responses. A positive response indicates that the respondent considers CFM to entail opportunity costs for the community."
  ) |>
  opt_align_table_header(align = "left") |>
  cols_align(align = "left",   columns = interviewee_role) |>
  cols_align(align = "center", columns = c(n, Opp_costs)) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(align = "center"),
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = interviewee_role)
  ) |>
  sub_missing()

NoteOpportunity costs – Individual interviewees – Model methods





To examine differences among individual interviewee roles in whether they reported that CFM entails opportunity costs for the community, we employed a generalised linear mixed model (GLMM) with a binomial response and random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We included interviewee role, interviewee gender and interviewee age as factors. To examine pairwise differences among interviewee roles, we employed Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data.opp.ind <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    empl = case_when(
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm == "yes"           ~ 1,
      economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm == "no"            ~ 0,
      str_starts(economic_transformation_opportunity_costs_of_cfm, "No_") ~ 0,
      TRUE                                                                 ~ NA_real_
    ),
    interviewee_gender = demo_information_interviewee_gender,
    interviewee_age    = demo_information_interviewee_age
  ) |>
  drop_na(empl) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M10 <- glmer(empl ~ interviewee_role + interviewee_gender + interviewee_age +
               (1 | province) + (1 | province_cfmg),
             data = test.data.opp.ind, family = binomial)

emm.M10      <- emmeans(M10, specs = ~ interviewee_role, type = "response")
pairwise.M10 <- pairs(emm.M10, adjust = "Tukey")

 dfo / NGO_rep                       0.397 0.202 Inf    1  -1.814  0.3650

Results are averaged over the levels of: interviewee_gender 
P value adjustment: tukey method for comparing a family of 5 estimates 
Tests are performed on the log odds ratio scale 




The types of opportunity costs identified are shown in Figure 24. Mentions of reduced access, land restrictions and land use conflict are by some margin the most frequently identified opportunity costs. However, it is also interesting that time costs and diverting community resources are also quite prevalent. CFM requires that community members meet which entails a time cost and community resources are diverted to managing the forest that might otherwise be spent on other activities, such as agriculture.


Code
prefix_oc <- "economic_transformation_main_opportunity_costs_experienced_"

oc_labels <- c(
  "reduced_access_to_forest_resources_e_g_firewood_building_materials"    = "Reduced access",
  "time_spent_on_cfm_activities_instead_of_income_generating_work"        = "Time cost",
  "conflict_with_other_land_resource_uses"                                = "Land use conflict",
  "restrictions_on_land_use_for_farming_or_other_activities"              = "Land restrictions",
  "economic_costs_from_diverting_community_resources_to_cfm"              = "Diverting resources"
)

full_oc_vars <- paste0(prefix_oc, names(oc_labels))

binary_oc <- cfmg |>
  select(all_of(full_oc_vars)) |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(. == 1, na.rm = TRUE))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "freq") |>
  mutate(word = oc_labels[sub(prefix_oc, "", variable, fixed = TRUE)]) |>
  filter(freq > 0) |>
  select(word, freq)

comment_oc <- cfmg |>
  select(text = economic_transformation_main_opportunity_costs_experienced_comment) |>
  filter(!is.na(text), trimws(text) != "") |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
  mutate(word = case_match(
    word,
    c("charcoal", "production", "prohibition", "stopped",
      "restriction", "restrictions") ~ "Charcoal",
    "chitemene"                        ~ "Chitemene",
    "grazing"                          ~ "Grazing",
    "timber"                           ~ "Timber",
    "logging"                          ~ "Logging",
    "fruits"                           ~ "Fruits",
    "medicine"                         ~ "Medicinal plants",
    .default                           = word
  )) |>
  filter(!word %in% c("reduced", "involved", "traditional",
                       "interference", "access", "source",
                       "villages", "moment")) |>
  count(word, name = "freq")

wc_oc <- bind_rows(binary_oc, comment_oc) |>
  group_by(word) |>
  summarise(freq = sum(freq), .groups = "drop")

set.seed(42)
ggplot(wc_oc, aes(label = word, size = freq, colour = freq)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud(eccentricity = 0.65, seed = 42) +
  scale_size(range = c(3, 18)) +
  scale_colour_gradient(low = "#F4A582", high = "#8B0000") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0))








Figure 24: Types of opportunity costs experienced under CFM.

title: Economic Transformation
text:
Discussion
Through the FGDs and the individual interviews, we investigated a diverse set of indicators of economic transformation. We covered access to natural resources for household use and sale, employment, income earning opportunities, including carbon finance and other sources of income, and opportunity costs.
Most FGD groups and individual interviewees indicated that CFM had not increased access to natural resources for either household use or sale. However, about a third of respondents considered that access to resources had improved under CFM. Given that the communities were involved in harvesting forest products before the instigation of CFM, it is not surprising that most respondents do not recognise any change. However, around one third of respondent groups and individuals felt that access had improved under CFM. This presumably reflects the formalisation of community rights to access forest resources.
A larger proportion of FGD groups considered that CFM had had a positive effect on full-time employment. However, the differences among different FGD types were highly significant. More community female groups suggested there was no change in employment and more community youth groups felt full-time formal employment opportunities had increased. We also showed that the numbers employed included many more men than women and, given that by far the most important employment roles were HFOs and Community Scouts, it is likely they also included a disproportionate number of youth, which would explain these differences in perception. Moreover, while CFM does appear to have increased formal employment, the numbers are quite paltry. However, this may reflect the fact that employment in forest product harvesting and processing is rarely formal or full-time. In short, while CMF does appear to have resulted in some new employment opportunities, these have been largely limited to HFO and Community Scout roles and have not benefited many women.
A large proportion of CFs in Zambia were formed on the promise of carbon dollars. However, only approximately one third of the CFs in our sample appeared to have benefited from carbon income at the time of the survey. The fact that a number of the FDG groups, especially youth groups, were unaware of the carbon agreement is concerning. It suggests that the carbon trading companies may have failed to conduct a proper FPIC process involving the entire community and that the CFMG Executives may not be communicating properly with their communities. Nevertheless, many carbon traders are clearly supporting communities. Most of the roles concern forest protection and agricultural extension for sustainable intensification, for example, climate smart agriculture. Both of these may be considered as essential to a carbon business plan. NTFPs and enterprise development were less often mentioned as a part of the carbon trader activities. This is perhaps an oversight on their part, because enhancing forest product value chains would increase the value of forest conservation for local communities.
The above results are backed up by the response to questions concerning other income earning opportunities. It seems CFM has not led to increased income from market linkages, alternative livelihoods, conservation revenue, forest product processing or ecotourism except for in a very few cases. Nevertheless, a wide variety of forest products clearly contribute to household use and some additional income. Although honey and mushrooms were most often identified as the most important source of additional income, another 10 NTFPs were mention as the most important at least once. However, these additional sources of income were identified in less than half of the CFs and only 13 identified a second rank. Thus, forest products are rarely being exploited as a source of additional income and if they are it is usually only one. Only a small number of CFMGs are partnering with an enterprise, such as an off-taker, and among those that do it almost exclusively concerns honey. Nonetheless, the communities identified a wide diversity of forest products that could potentially be harvested and processed to provide an additional source of income. Prominent among these were timber and wild fruit, as well as mushrooms and honey. In some cases timber harvesting may conflict with a carbon business plans, but not if plantations were established. The potential of CFM to support forest product enterprises is clearly massively under appreciated.
Unfortunately, the information on incomes from CFM were not very informative. Some clearly included income from carbon while most did not and it seems that the CFMG Executives have little understanding of the value of forest products being harvested from their CFs. To get a more accurate picture of these values a more in depth PEN type survey is recommended.
The local communities identified a number of opportunity costs to CFM. Most of these pertained to activities that are precluded, such as those involving forest clearance. However, many groups also identified other opportunity costs, such as time costs and diverted resources. Recognition of opportunity costs is important to understand the cost-benefits of CFM for communities.
Overall, a positive picture of economic transformation emerges from the responses of the FGDs and individual interviews. While changes in indicators, such as employment or additional income from forest products, many not appear substantial, they are in a positive direction. Most CFMGs will also receive carbon income, even if this has only benefited around one third of CFMGs so far. However, what is also evident is that CFMG business plans are over-reliant on just carbon or sometimes carbon and honey. Substantial opportunities for developing much more diversfied business plans exist. These could include a range of different NTFPs and potentially also timber. In addition to simply increasing income earning opportunities, diversified business plans would also provide a buffer against fluctuating commodity prices and because different products are harvested at different times of the year, they can help stabilise income throughout the year as well.

title: Equity and Inclusion
text: The overall picture for equity and inclusion is broadly positive, though with important caveats. A substantial majority of FGD groups reported that the benefits of CFM are being shared equitably and that women benefit from CFM without disproportionately suffering opportunity costs. Notably, a majority of community female FGD groups themselves agreed that women have benefited from CFM. Carbon benefit-sharing agreements between carbon companies and communities are dominated by revenue sharing, while employment and livelihood support components are less commonly included. Responsibility for carbon income sharing decisions within communities was roughly equally split between instances where the CFMG executive makes the decisions and those where the whole CFMG is involved. Importantly, community female and youth FGD groups were significantly more likely than executive and male FGD groups to report inequitable benefit sharing, inadequate participation of women in decision making, and a lack of transparency in benefit sharing. In some CFMGs, community youth groups also identified issues with elite capture and marginalisation of youth. This common divergence in perspective between community members and CFMG executives is a concerning pattern suggesting that a minority of CFMGs have real equity problems that are not visible from the executive’s perspective. Poor transparency in benefit sharing and the non-existence of grievance and redress mechanisms were also noted for many CFMGs. Without functioning formal channels through which equity concerns can be raised and addressed, inequitable outcomes risk going undetected and uncorrected. Strengthening these governance mechanisms is essential if CFM is to deliver equitable outcomes for all community members, including women, youth and other vulnerable groups.

title: Equity and Inclusion
text:
Introduction
In this chapter, we consider the equitable sharing of benefits arising from community forest management (CFM) and what the impacts of CFM are on vulnerable groups.

title: Equity and Inclusion
text:
Benefit sharing
We asked FGD groups whether the benefits of CFM were equitably distributed (Figure 1). On the one hand, a substantial majority of responses across the FGD groups asserted that benefits were being shared equitably. The proportion of community female and community youth groups that felt that benefits were not being shared equitably was significantly high than for the CFMG executive and community male FGD groups. Moreover, even among the CFMG executive and community male groups, there were some that agreed that benefits were not being shared equitably.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    response = economic_transformation_benefits_equity_across_groups,
    response = case_match(response,
                          "yes" ~ "Yes",
                          "no" ~ "No",
                          .default = NA),
    response = factor(response, levels = c("Yes", "No")),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female", "Executive")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(response) |>
  count(interview_type, response) |>
  group_by(interview_type) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(y = interview_type, x = prop, fill = response)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(prop >= 0.05, n, "")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    size = 3.5, fontface = "bold", colour = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = 0.01),
    name   = "Proportion of groups"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Yes" = "#2A9D8F", "No" = "#E07B54"),
    guide  = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)
  ) +
  labs(y = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.title.x       = element_text(size = rel(1), colour = "grey40"),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    legend.text        = element_text(size = rel(1)),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )








Figure 1: Are the benefits of CFM are equitably shared? The length of each bar segment represents the proportion of groups in that category. The numbers superimposed on the bars represent the actual number of groups in each category.











NoteEquity - FGDs - Model methods





To examine differences among FGD types in whether they agreed the benefits of CFM were equitably shared, we employed a generalised linear mixed model (GLMM) with a binomial response and random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We used FGD type as the factor. We examined the significance of pairwise differences among FGD types using Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    empl = economic_transformation_benefits_equity_across_groups,
    empl = case_match(empl,
                      "yes" ~ 1,
                      "no" ~ 0,
                      .default = NA),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Executive", "Female", "Male", "Youth")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(empl) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M1 <- glmer(empl ~ interview_type + (1 | province) + (1 | province_cfmg),
            data = test.data, family = binomial)

emm.M1      <- emmeans(M1, specs = ~ interview_type, type = "response")
pairwise.M1 <- pairs(emm.M1, adjust = "Tukey")

Individual interviewees were also asked whether the benefits of CFM were equitably shared. Table 1 shows the percentage reporting this broken down by interviewee role. Again a substantial majority of respondents (83% - 92%) thought that benefits were being shared equitably, and the differences among respondents were not significant. However, there was still minority of respondents that did not think benefits were being equitably shared. This suggests that there are some CFs where the benefit sharing arrangements are not optimal or appropriate arrangements are not being followed.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  select(
    interviewee_role,
    empl = economic_transformation_benefits_equity_across_groups
  ) |>
  mutate(
    interviewee_role = case_match(
      as.character(interviewee_role),
      "cfmg_member"      ~ "Community member",
      "cfmg_exec_member" ~ "CFMG executive member",
      "trad_leader"      ~ "Traditional leader",
      "dfo"              ~ "District Forestry Officer",
      "NGO_rep"          ~ "NGO representative"
    ),
    interviewee_role = factor(interviewee_role, levels = c(
      "Community member", "CFMG executive member", "Traditional leader",
      "District Forestry Officer", "NGO representative"
    )),
    empl = case_match(empl,
                      "yes" ~ 1,
                      "no" ~ 0,
                      .default = NA)
    
  ) |>
  group_by(interviewee_role) |>
  summarise(
    n          = n(),
    Equity = mean(empl, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups    = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(interviewee_role) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    interviewee_role = "Role",
    n                = "n",
    Equity       = "Agree benefits are equitably shared"
  ) |>
  fmt_percent(
    columns  = Equity,
    decimals = 0
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Equitable sharing of benefits, as reported by individual interviewees",
    subtitle = "Values represent the % of positive responses. n = the total number of interview responses. A positive response indicates that benefits of CFM are equitably shared."
  ) |>
  opt_align_table_header(align = "left") |>
  cols_align(align = "left",   columns = interviewee_role) |>
  cols_align(align = "center", columns = c(n, Equity)) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(align = "center"),
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = interviewee_role)
  ) |>
  sub_missing()

NoteEquity - Individual interviewees - Model methods





To examine differences among individual interviewee roles in whether they agreed the benefits of CFM were equitably shared, we employed a generalised linear mixed model (GLMM) with a binomial response and random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We included interviewee role, interviewee gender and interviewee age as factors. To examine pairwise differences among interviewee roles, we employed Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    empl               = economic_transformation_benefits_equity_across_groups,
    empl = case_match(empl,
                      "yes" ~ 1,
                      "no" ~ 0,
                      .default = NA),
    interviewee_gender = demo_information_interviewee_gender,
    interviewee_age    = demo_information_interviewee_age
  ) |>
  drop_na(empl) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M2 <- glmer(empl ~ interviewee_role + interviewee_gender + interviewee_age +
              (1 | province) + (1 | province_cfmg),
            data = test.data, family = binomial)

emm.M2      <- emmeans(M2, specs = ~ interviewee_role, type = "response")
pairwise.M2 <- pairs(emm.M2, adjust = "Tukey")

title: Equity and Inclusion
text:
Carbon income benefit sharing

Company-community arrangements
CFMG executive FGDs reported on the components were included in their carbon finance benefit-sharing arrangement with the carbon trading enterprise (Figure 2). Clearly the agreements are dominated by revenue sharing, with relatively few also containing agreements on employment, infrastructure development and livelihood support.


Code
prefix_bs <- "economic_transformation_benefit_sharing_arrangement_company_community_"

bs_labels <- c(
  "revenue_sharing_percentage_of_carbon_finance_proceeds_given_to_the_community"              = "Revenue sharing",
  "employment_opportunities_for_community_members"                                             = "Employment",
  "investment_in_community_infrastructure_e_g_schools_clinics_roads"                          = "Infrastructure investment",
  "support_for_sustainable_livelihoods_e_g_alternative_income_generating_activities_training_programs" = "Livelihood support"
)

full_bs_vars <- paste0(prefix_bs, names(bs_labels))

cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "cfmg_executive") |>
  select(all_of(full_bs_vars)) |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(. == 1, na.rm = TRUE))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "n") |>
  mutate(label = factor(bs_labels[sub(prefix_bs, "", variable, fixed = TRUE)],
                        levels = rev(bs_labels))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = label)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2A9D8F", width = 0.55) +

    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)),
    name   = "Number of CFMGs"
  ) +

    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),

Figure 2: Components included in the carbon finance benefit-sharing arrangement between the carbon company and the community as reported by CFMG executive FGDs. The sample includes only those CFMGs that stated they had an agreement with a carbon trading company.







Within-community sharing
We also asked FGD groups about the carbon income sharing arrangements within the community. We first asked who is responsible for the decision-making (Figure 3) and then we asked on what principles the benefit sharing was based (Figure 4). As can be seen there is broad agreement among the FGD groups on who makes the decision about the caron benefit sharing arrangements. In approximately half the CFs the CFMG executive is responsible for the decision, while in the other half the entire CFMG is responsible.


Code
prefix_bsc <- "economic_transformation_benefit_sharing_arrangement_carbon_income_"

decision_labels <- c(
  "decided_by_the_whole_cfmg"     = "Whole CFMG",
  "decided_by_the_cfmg_executive" = "Executive decision"
)

full_decision_vars <- paste0(prefix_bsc, names(decision_labels))

cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type %in% c("cfmg_executive", "cfmg_female",
                                "cfmg_male", "cfmg_youth")) |>
  mutate(group = factor(
    case_match(
      interview_type,
      "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
      "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
      "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
      "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
    ),
    levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female", "Executive")
  )) |>
  select(group, all_of(full_decision_vars)) |>
  pivot_longer(all_of(full_decision_vars), names_to = "variable", values_to = "val") |>
  filter(val == 1) |>
  mutate(mechanism = factor(
    decision_labels[sub(prefix_bsc, "", variable, fixed = TRUE)],
    levels = decision_labels
  )) |>
  count(group, mechanism) |>
  ggplot(aes(y = group, x = n, fill = mechanism)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = n),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    size = 3.5, colour = "white", fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Whole CFMG"         = "#2A9D8F",
      "Executive decision" = "#E07B54"
    ),
    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05)),
    name   = "Number of FGDs"
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(expand = expansion(add = 0.5)) +
  labs(y = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 18) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(colour = "grey20"),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = 14, colour = "grey20"),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    legend.text        = element_text(size = rel(0.85)),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 15, 10, 10)
  )








Figure 3: Who decides how carbon income is shared within the community by FGD group. The length of the segments is equal to the number of times that option was mentioned by the FGD group. Note: for 12 CFs the benefit-sharing arrangement is defined in the CFMG constitution.

According to the responses of the CFMG executives, seven CFs shared benefits acccording to members contribution to CFM while six shared them equally among the community households. Only two said that consideration of marginalised or vulnerable groups was a consideration. The other FGD groups were broadly similar in their responses and differences may be due to their knowledge of the arrangements, rather than actual differences in the arrangements.

title: Equity and Inclusion
text:
Gender equity
We asked FGD groups whether women benefit from CFM (Figure 6).


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    response = equity_and_inclusion_women_benefit_from_cfm,
    response = case_match(response,
                          "yes" ~ "Yes",
                          "no" ~ "No",
                          .default = NA),
    response = factor(response, levels = c("Yes", "No")),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female", "Executive")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(response) |>
  count(interview_type, response) |>
  group_by(interview_type) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(y = interview_type, x = prop, fill = response)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(prop >= 0.05, n, "")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    size = 3.5, fontface = "bold", colour = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = 0.01),
    name   = "Proportion of groups"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Yes" = "#2A9D8F", "No" = "#E07B54"),
    guide  = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)
  ) +
  labs(y = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.title.x       = element_text(size = rel(1), colour = "grey40"),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    legend.text        = element_text(size = rel(1)),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )








Figure 6: Do women benefit from CFM? The length of each bar segment represents the proportion of groups in that category. The numbers superimposed on the bars represent the actual number of groups in each category.











NoteWomen benefit from CFM - FGDs - Model methods





To examine differences among FGD types in whether they agreed that women benefit from CFM, we employed a generalised linear mixed model (GLMM) with a binomial response and random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We used FGD type as the factor. We examined the significance of pairwise differences among FGD types using Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    empl = equity_and_inclusion_women_benefit_from_cfm,
    empl = case_match(empl,
                      "yes" ~ 1,
                      "no" ~ 0,
                      .default = NA),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Executive", "Female", "Male", "Youth")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(empl) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M3 <- glmer(empl ~ interview_type + (1 | province) + (1 | province_cfmg),
            data = test.data, family = binomial)

emm.M3      <- emmeans(M3, specs = ~ interview_type, type = "response")
pairwise.M3 <- pairs(emm.M3, adjust = "Tukey")

Individual interviewees were also asked whether women benefit from CFM. Table 2 shows the percentage reporting this broken down by interviewee role.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  select(
    interviewee_role,
    empl = equity_and_inclusion_women_benefit_from_cfm
  ) |>
  mutate(
    interviewee_role = case_match(
      as.character(interviewee_role),
      "cfmg_member"      ~ "Community member",
      "cfmg_exec_member" ~ "CFMG executive member",
      "trad_leader"      ~ "Traditional leader",
      "dfo"              ~ "District Forestry Officer",
      "NGO_rep"          ~ "NGO representative"
    ),
    interviewee_role = factor(interviewee_role, levels = c(
      "Community member", "CFMG executive member", "Traditional leader",
      "District Forestry Officer", "NGO representative"
    )),
    empl = case_match(empl,
                      "yes" ~ 1,
                      "no" ~ 0,
                      .default = NA)
  ) |>
  group_by(interviewee_role) |>
  summarise(
    n             = n(),
    Women_benefit = mean(empl, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups       = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(interviewee_role) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    interviewee_role = "Role",
    n                = "n",
    Women_benefit    = "Agree women benefit from CFM"
  ) |>
  fmt_percent(
    columns  = Women_benefit,
    decimals = 0
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Women benefit from CFM, as reported by individual interviewees",
    subtitle = "Values represent the % of positive responses. n = the total number of interview responses. A positive response indicates that women benefit from CFM."
  ) |>
  opt_align_table_header(align = "left") |>
  cols_align(align = "left",   columns = interviewee_role) |>
  cols_align(align = "center", columns = c(n, Women_benefit)) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(align = "center"),
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = interviewee_role)
  ) |>
  sub_missing()

NoteWomen benefit from CFM - Individual interviewees - Model methods





To examine differences among individual interviewee roles in whether they agreed that women benefit from CFM, we employed a generalised linear mixed model (GLMM) with a binomial response and random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We included interviewee role, interviewee gender and interviewee age as factors. To examine pairwise differences among interviewee roles, we employed Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>

    empl               = equity_and_inclusion_women_benefit_from_cfm,
    empl = case_match(empl,
                      "yes" ~ 1,
                      "no" ~ 0,
                      .default = NA),
    interviewee_gender = demo_information_interviewee_gender,
    interviewee_age    = demo_information_interviewee_age
  ) |>
  drop_na(empl) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M4 <- glmer(empl ~ interviewee_role + interviewee_gender + interviewee_age +
              (1 | province) + (1 | province_cfmg),
            data = test.data, family = binomial)

emm.M4      <- emmeans(M4, specs = ~ interviewee_role, type = "response")
pairwise.M4 <- pairs(emm.M4, adjust = "Tukey")

Women disproportionately suffer opportunity costs
We asked FGD groups whether women disproportionately suffer the opportunity costs of CFM (Figure 7).


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    response = equity_and_inclusion_women_disproportionally_suffer_opportunity_costs,
    response = case_match(response,
                          "yes" ~ "Yes",
                          "no" ~ "No",
                          .default = NA),
    response = factor(response, levels = c("Yes", "No")),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female", "Executive")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(response) |>

Transparency in benefit sharing
We asked FGD groups whether there is transparency in CFM benefit sharing (Figure 9).


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    response = equity_and_inclusion_transparency_in_benefit_sharing,
    response = case_match(response,
                          "yes" ~ "Yes",
                          "no" ~ "No",
                          .default = NA),
    response = factor(response, levels = c("Yes", "No")),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female", "Executive")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(response) |>
  count(interview_type, response) |>

  ggplot(aes(y = interview_type, x = prop, fill = response)) +

NoteTransparency in benefit sharing - FGDs - Model methods





To examine differences among FGD types in whether they agreed there is transparency in CFM benefit sharing, we employed a generalised linear mixed model (GLMM) with a binomial response and random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We used FGD type as the factor. We examined the significance of pairwise differences among FGD types using Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    empl = equity_and_inclusion_transparency_in_benefit_sharing,
    empl = case_match(empl,
                      "yes" ~ 1,
                      "no" ~ 0,
                      .default = NA),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Executive", "Female", "Male", "Youth")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(empl) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M9 <- glmer(empl ~ interview_type + (1 | province) + (1 | province_cfmg),
            data = test.data, family = binomial)

emm.M9      <- emmeans(M9, specs = ~ interview_type, type = "response")
pairwise.M9 <- pairs(emm.M9, adjust = "Tukey")

Individual interviewees were also asked whether there is transparency in CFM benefit sharing. Table 5 shows the percentage reporting this broken down by interviewee role.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  select(
    interviewee_role,
    empl = equity_and_inclusion_transparency_in_benefit_sharing
  ) |>
  mutate(
    interviewee_role = case_match(
      as.character(interviewee_role),
      "cfmg_member"      ~ "Community member",
      "cfmg_exec_member" ~ "CFMG executive member",
      "trad_leader"      ~ "Traditional leader",
      "dfo"              ~ "District Forestry Officer",
      "NGO_rep"          ~ "NGO representative"
    ),
    interviewee_role = factor(interviewee_role, levels = c(
      "Community member", "CFMG executive member", "Traditional leader",
      "District Forestry Officer", "NGO representative"
    )),
    empl = case_match(empl,
                      "yes" ~ 1,
                      "no" ~ 0,
                      .default = NA)
  ) |>
  group_by(interviewee_role) |>
  summarise(
    n            = n(),
    Transparency = mean(empl, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups      = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(interviewee_role) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    interviewee_role = "Role",
    n                = "n",
    Transparency     = "Agree there is transparency in benefit sharing"
  ) |>
  fmt_percent(
    columns  = Transparency,
    decimals = 0
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Transparency in CFM benefit sharing, as reported by individual interviewees",
    subtitle = "Values represent the % of positive responses. n = the total number of interview responses."
  ) |>
  opt_align_table_header(align = "left") |>
  cols_align(align = "left",   columns = interviewee_role) |>
  cols_align(align = "center", columns = c(n, Transparency)) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(align = "center"),
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = interviewee_role)
  ) |>
  sub_missing()

NoteTransparency in benefit sharing - Individual interviewees - Model methods





To examine differences among individual interviewee roles in whether they agreed there is transparency in CFM benefit sharing, we employed a generalised linear mixed model (GLMM) with a binomial response and random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We included interviewee role, interviewee gender and interviewee age as factors. To examine pairwise differences among interviewee roles, we employed Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    empl               = equity_and_inclusion_transparency_in_benefit_sharing,
    empl = case_match(empl,
                      "yes" ~ 1,
                      "no" ~ 0,
                      .default = NA),
    interviewee_gender = demo_information_interviewee_gender,
    interviewee_age    = demo_information_interviewee_age
  ) |>
  drop_na(empl) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M10 <- glmer(empl ~ interviewee_role + interviewee_gender + interviewee_age +
               (1 | province) + (1 | province_cfmg),
             data = test.data, family = binomial)

emm.M10      <- emmeans(M10, specs = ~ interviewee_role, type = "response")
pairwise.M10 <- pairs(emm.M10, adjust = "Tukey")

We also examined which mechanisms are being used to achieve transparency in benefit sharing among the community female, male, and youth FGDs (Figure 10).


Code
prefix_trans <- "equity_and_inclusion_mechanisms_for_transparency_fairness_"

trans_labels <- c(
  "community_meetings_to_discuss_benefit_sharing_decisions"                                                    = "Community meetings",
  "written_agreements_or_guidelines_outlining_benefit_distribution_rules"                                     = "Written agreements",
  "memos"                                                                                                      = "Memos",
  "financial_reports_or_audits_shared_with_community_members"                                                 = "Financial reports/audits",
  "inclusive_representation_of_women_youth_and_marginalized_groups_in_decision_making"                        = "Inclusive representation",
  "external_oversight_by_government_agencies_ngos_or_independent_bodies"                                     = "External oversight",
  "grievance_and_dispute_resolution_mechanisms_for_complaints_or_conflicts"                                   = "Grievance mechanisms",
  "needs_assessment"                                                                                           = "Needs assessment"
)

full_trans_vars <- paste0(prefix_trans, names(trans_labels))

cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type %in% c("cfmg_female", "cfmg_male", "cfmg_youth")) |>
  mutate(group = factor(
    case_match(
      interview_type,
      "cfmg_female" ~ "Female",
      "cfmg_male"   ~ "Male",
      "cfmg_youth"  ~ "Youth"
    ),
    levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female")
  )) |>
  select(group, all_of(full_trans_vars)) |>
  pivot_longer(all_of(full_trans_vars), names_to = "variable", values_to = "val") |>
  filter(val == 1) |>
  mutate(mechanism = factor(
    trans_labels[sub(prefix_trans, "", variable, fixed = TRUE)],
    levels = rev(trans_labels)
  )) |>
  count(group, mechanism) |>
  complete(group, mechanism, fill = list(n = 0)) |>
  ggplot(aes(y = mechanism, x = n, fill = group)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.75), width = 0.65, colour = "white") +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(n > 0, n, "")),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.75),
    hjust = -0.3, size = 3.5, colour = "grey30"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Female" = "#2A9D8F",
      "Male"   = "#57B8B0",
      "Youth"  = "#E9C46A"
    ),
    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)),
    name   = "Number of FGDs"
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = \(x) str_wrap(x, width = 25), expand = expansion(add = 0.6)) +
  labs(y = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(colour = "grey20"),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = 14, colour = "grey20"),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )

      "dfo"              ~ "District Forestry Officer",
      "NGO_rep"          ~ "NGO representative"
    ),
    interviewee_role = factor(interviewee_role, levels = c(
      "Community member", "CFMG executive member", "Traditional leader",
      "District Forestry Officer", "NGO representative"
    )),
    empl = case_match(empl,
                      "yes" ~ 1,
                      "no" ~ 0,
                      .default = NA)
  ) |>
  group_by(interviewee_role) |>
  summarise(
    n         = n(),
    Grievance = mean(empl, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups   = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(interviewee_role) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    interviewee_role = "Role",
    n                = "n",
    Grievance        = "Grievance and redress mechanism"
  ) |>
  fmt_percent(
    columns  = Grievance,
    decimals = 0
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Grievance and redress mechanism, as reported by individual interviewees",
    subtitle = "Values represent the % of positive responses. n = the total number of interview responses."
  ) |>
  opt_align_table_header(align = "left") |>
  cols_align(align = "left",   columns = interviewee_role) |>
  cols_align(align = "center", columns = c(n, Grievance)) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(align = "center"),
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = interviewee_role)
  ) |>
  sub_missing()

NoteGrievance and redress - Individual interviewees - Model methods





To examine differences among individual interviewee roles in whether they reported the CFMG has a grievance and redress mechanism, we employed a generalised linear mixed model (GLMM) with a binomial response and random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We included interviewee role, interviewee gender and interviewee age as factors. To examine pairwise differences among interviewee roles, we employed Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    empl               = equity_and_inclusion_grievance_redress_mechanism,
    empl = case_match(empl,
                      "yes" ~ 1,
                      "no" ~ 0,
                      .default = NA),
    interviewee_gender = demo_information_interviewee_gender,
    interviewee_age    = demo_information_interviewee_age
  ) |>
  drop_na(empl) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M12 <- glmer(empl ~ interviewee_role + interviewee_gender + interviewee_age +
               (1 | province) + (1 | province_cfmg),
             data = test.data, family = binomial)

emm.M12      <- emmeans(M12, specs = ~ interviewee_role, type = "response")
pairwise.M12 <- pairs(emm.M12, adjust = "Tukey")

We also examined which mechanisms are being used to achieve grievance and redress among the community female, male, and youth FGDs (Figure 12).

griev_labels <- c(
  "community_meetings_or_forums_for_conflict_resolution"  = "Community meetings/forums",
  "mediation_by_traditional_leaders"                      = "Mediation by traditional leaders",
  "formal_grievance_committees_within_the_cfm_group"      = "Formal grievance committees",
  "legal_channels_local_courts_government_agencies"       = "Legal channels",
  "external_mediation_by_ngos_or_third_parties"           = "External mediation (NGOs)",
  "anonymous_reporting_mechanisms_suggestion_boxes"        = "Anonymous reporting",
  "stipulated_in_constitution"                            = "Stipulated in constitution",
  "stipulated_in_redress_document"                        = "Stipulated in redress document"
)

full_griev_vars <- paste0(prefix_griev, names(griev_labels))

cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type %in% c("cfmg_female", "cfmg_male", "cfmg_youth")) |>
  mutate(group = factor(
    case_match(
      interview_type,
      "cfmg_female" ~ "Female",
      "cfmg_male"   ~ "Male",
      "cfmg_youth"  ~ "Youth"

    levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female")

Barriers to poorer community members
We asked the community female, male, and youth FGDs to identify the barriers that prevent poorer community members from benefiting from CFM (Figure 14).


Code
prefix_b <- "equity_and_inclusion_challenges_for_poorer_community_members_"

barrier_labels <- c(
  "lack_or_limited_cfm_benefits"                                                         = "Limited CFM benefits",
  "limited_access_to_information_about_cfm_opportunities_and_benefits"                   = "Limited information",
  "lack_of_financial_and_other_resources_to_participate_in_cfm_activities"              = "Lack of resources",
  "exclusion_from_decision_making_processes_within_the_cfm_group"                       = "Excluded from decisions",
  "unequal_distribution_of_benefits_among_community_members"                             = "Unequal benefit distribution",
  "limited_access_to_training_or_capacity_building_opportunities"                       = "Limited training",
  "high_opportunity_costs_time_labor_or_resources_lost_by_participating_in_cfm"        = "High opportunity costs",
  "social_or_cultural_barriers_discrimination_based_on_gender_age_or_status"          = "Social/cultural barriers",
  "physical_distance_from_cfm_activities_or_resources"                                  = "Physical distance",
  "lack_of_trust_in_cfm_leadership_management_or_benefit_sharing_mechanisms"          = "No trust in leadership",
  "lack_of_interest_in_cfm_activities"                                                  = "Lack of interest",
  "cfma_access_restrictions"                                                             = "CFMA access restrictions",
  "influence_by_stronger_indiv"                                                          = "Stronger individual influence",
  "human_wildlife_conflicts"                                                             = "Human-wildlife conflicts",
  "selfishness"                                                                          = "Selfishness",
  "interference_from_govt_and_traditional_leadership"                                   = "Govt/traditional leader interference"
)

full_b_vars <- paste0(prefix_b, names(barrier_labels))

# Frequency counts from binary checkbox variables
barrier_freq <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type %in% c("cfmg_female", "cfmg_male", "cfmg_youth")) |>
  select(all_of(full_b_vars)) |>
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(. == 1, na.rm = TRUE))) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "freq") |>
  mutate(word = barrier_labels[sub(prefix_b, "", variable, fixed = TRUE)]) |>
  filter(freq > 0) |>
  select(word, freq)

# Word frequencies from open-ended comments
comment_freq <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type %in% c("cfmg_female", "cfmg_male", "cfmg_youth")) |>
  select(comment = equity_and_inclusion_challenges_for_poorer_community_members_comment) |>
  drop_na(comment) |>
  unnest_tokens(word, comment) |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
  filter(nchar(word) > 2, !word %in% c(
    "cfm", "cfmg", "forest", "limited", "lack",
    "youths", "visible", "accessing", "people", "poorer", "community", "coming"
  )) |>
  count(word, name = "freq") |>
  filter(freq > 1)

# Combine checkbox labels and comment words
barriers_all <- bind_rows(barrier_freq, comment_freq) |>
  group_by(word) |>
  summarise(freq = sum(freq), .groups = "drop")

set.seed(42)
ggplot(barriers_all, aes(label = word, size = freq, colour = freq)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud(eccentricity = 0.65, seed = 42) +
  scale_size(range = c(3, 22)) +
  scale_colour_gradient(low = "#F4A582", high = "#8B0000") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0))
