title: Equity and Inclusion
text:
Discussion
Through the FGDs and individual interviews, we investigated a wide range of equity and inclusion indicators, covering benefit sharing, gender equity, transparency, grievance and redress, and poverty impacts.
The most striking and consistent finding is a divergence in perspective by FGD group. Across virtually every indicator — equitable benefit sharing, women benefiting from CFM, women suffering disproportionate opportunity costs, women’s participation in decision making, and transparency in benefit sharing — community female and youth groups were significantly more likely than CFMG executive and male groups to report negative outcomes. Given that CFMG executives and community males may typically be better informed about formal governance arrangements, it is possible that this divergence reflects an information asymmetry rather than disagreement about conditions. However, it may also reflect that the benefits and costs of CFM are being distributed unevenly, in ways that are visible to female and youth community members but not acknowledged — or perhaps not noticed — by those in the CFMG executives. It is also possible that these differences in perspective reflect elite capture.
Notwithstanding these these issues, the overall picture is more positive than might have been expected. A clear majority of all FGD groups — including community female groups — reported that benefits are being shared equitably and that women benefit from CFM. Most female groups also stated that women do not disproportionately suffer opportunity costs and that women participate in CFM decision making. This is somewhat surprising, given that natural resource management decisions in Zambia are traditionally the purview of male community members. It may be that the formal governance structures mandated by CFM — CFMG elections, executive positions with female representation, and documented benefit-sharing arrangements — are providing women with a degree of recognition and voice that they would not otherwise have had.
Carbon benefit-sharing agreements between companies and communities are almost entirely structured around revenue sharing. Employment, infrastructure investment and livelihood support are less commonly included in these agreements. While revenue sharing provides a direct financial return, it means communities that have not yet received a carbon payment have little to show for CFM. Diversifying the components of company-community agreements could broaden both the distribution of benefits and community buy-in.
The reported poverty impacts of CFM — primarily increased household incomes and new income-generating activities — are broadly consistent with the economic transformation chapter findings. However, barriers to poorer community members benefiting remain, including limited access to resources, capital and markets. The solutions communities themselves proposed — financial support, targeted programmes for marginalised groups, and better governance — align closely with the governance and economic transformation recommendations identified elsewhere in this report.
In conclusion, the equity and inclusion outcomes of CFM in Zambia are broadly positive but uneven. The divergence between female and youth perspectives and those of CFMG executives is the most important finding of this chapter. It signals that equity problems in a subset of CFMGs are likely more widespread than executive-level data alone would suggest, and that strengthening transparency, grievance mechanisms and community-wide engagement is essential to ensuring CFM benefits reach all community members equitably.

title: Recommendations
text: Derived from our experiences with this project, we make the following recommendations. This is not an exhaustive list, and moreover we recognise that many of these actions may be in hand to some degree. However, we attempt to cover key changes that need to be made to improve CFM implementation in Zambia. We have structured these recommendations around the same four domains that the report focuses on, namely governance, SFM, economic transformation and equity and inclusion.

title: Recommendations
text:
Governance

DFOs should visit each CFMG once per quarter and support the CFMGs in developing and implementing their workplans. They should be applying the “Checklists – CFM Step 7: Joint Monitoring, Evaluation and Lesson Learning”. Key Performance Indicators for DFOs should be adjusted to reflect their role in the delivery of CFM support.
The FD should establish a key unit at HQ to support CFM implementation nationally. This unit should focus on programmes to support CFM including capacity development for forestry staff.
Budgets need to be made available for regular DFO visits to CFMGs. If it is not possible to assign sufficient budget from the allocation to the Forestry Department, the necessary funds could come from the government funds raised from carbon, if a proportion of these were ear-marked to support CFM. The justification for this would be that it is a necessary part of the government’s carbon business plan to support CFMG governance.
In line with the “Checklists – CFM Step 7: Joint Monitoring, Evaluation and Lesson Learning” implementation, increased standardisation of important CFMG processes would expedite CFMG processes, simplify monitoring and reduced the risk of poor outcomes. Standardisation should cover: (i) election processes, tenure and term limits for CFMG members and executive positions; (ii) financial processes, including opening bank accounts, maintaining receipt books, payments to employees including HFOs, and gratuities to CFMG members and executives for participating in activities; (iii) reporting requirements, including presentation of annual financial and activity reports to community members; (iv) grievance and redress mechanisms; and (v) benefit sharing arrangements for both carbon and other CFMG income. Several of these are already in the standard CFM agreement, but implementation is patchy, and many could be standardised to a greater degree. Where more flexibility is required, the FD should publish guidelines, such as for different permitting arrangements and user-group management, which always require some local tailoring.
To improve transparency, require publishing of decisions made at CFMG meetings and posting of these around the community.
Require annual implementation plans and budgets and require that the DFOs and CFMG executives sign off on these by a specified date and submit them to the FD database.
Require registration and permitting of all forest user-groups, even if these are community groups and there is no intention to charge a permit fee. Formalising the forest use and agreeing on management protocols, such as start dates and off-take levels, is essential for SFM.
Transition the current CFM database into a digital management dashboard for CFM monitoring that can be integrated with digital data collection tools and file uploads. Ideally, it would have an associated App that would facilitate recording of data on common processes, such as CFMG meetings and elections, permits, workplans, budgets, annual reports, and so on.

title: Recommendations
text:
Sustainable forest management

DFOs and CFMGs should identify key SFM activities within the management plan, such a boundary marking, fire monitoring and patrolling, and develop workplans - with completion dates for key milestones - for each within the overall CFMG workplan.
All CFMGs should identify restoration sites within the CF in forest management plans. CFM is a key mechanism for the government to deliver on restoration commitments but currently this is not being adequately assessed. Any area that is / was substantially degraded when the CF was established may be considered a restoration site. Within restoration sites, the management plan should identify areas for (i) natural regeneration (protection only), (ii) planting with indigenous species, and (iii) planting with exotic species.
Establish community based monitoring systems for NTFPs. These systems should be developed centrally and standardised. As above, data can be entered into a FD managed digital management tool, with a dashboard for community viewing.
Consider a small plantation programme or outgrowers schemes for CFs for timber or sustainable charcoal production. These could be established on deforested land within the CF and would contribute to Zambia’s plantation and timber production targets.

title: Recommendations
text:
Economic transformation

Require the production and submission to FD of business plans for CFs. The format should be standardised to facilitate the maintenance of quality and so that comparable data can be extracted from them.
Require the monitoring of harvested quantities of forest products for household use and for sale.
Require recording of employment statistics, such as types of jobs and hours worked per month
Support the National Forest Commodities Association and help establish them as a national knowledge hub on forest product management, value addition, and marketing. Help promote forest product innovation and connect producers with buyers by organising forest product fairs / exhibitions at the annual agricultural shows at provincial and national levels.
To address the lack of diversity in forest product economic activities, require / encourage carbon traders and NGOs working with CFMGs to engage in a minimum of three forest product value chains in each CF
Develop programme for community-based eco-tourism through CFs and their intersection with national heritage sites. This should build on experiences at existing sites, such as M’panshya Campsite, Rufunsa and others like the community campsites run by Frankfurt Zoological Society.
Consider promoting Forest Stewardship Certification (FSC) for CFMGs. FSC certification can improve carbon credit values by up to 100% and open international markets for NTFPs. In addition, the external auditing that is required for FSC certification imposes a high quality of SMF which will help FD reach its goals for SFM and enhance the reputation of Zambia forest management.

title: Recommendations
text:
Equity and inclusion

Require identification of opportunity costs of CF and the community members most affected in the forest management plan
Require consideration of vulnerable groups in the designation of forest user-groups and in the benefit sharing arrangements
Encourage development of women’s forest product enterprises through the NGOs and carbon trader support, as well as through the National Forest Commodities Association

title: Methods
text: We used a stratified random approach for selecting CFs for the survey. First, we identified eight provinces for sampling, omitting Luapula and Copperbelt provinces which did not have many CFs. Our intention was to select ten CFs per province stratified by size and age (preference for older CFMAs). However, we found that three of the selected provinces had fewer than ten CFs. Hence, to maintain the number of CFs at 80, we selected 12 CFs from provinces that had more than ten. We established a cut off establishment date of 1st Jan 2024, so that all selected CFs were at least 1 yr old. In addition, we were not able to use CF size as a selection criteria, because of the low number CFs in several of the provinces. Within provinces, we selected our sample randomly from among the CFs that were at least 1 yr old.

Community forest management (CFM) has emerged over the past five decades as a dominant paradigm in forest governance, grounded in the recognition that local communities — as the de facto managers over large areas of the world’s forests — are best placed to sustainably manage the resources on which they depend. At its core, CFM involves the formal transfer of management authority and resource use rights from state institutions to local communities, alongside recognition of their right to benefit from forest resources (Charnley and Poe 2007; FAO 1978). Although early programmes in the 1970s and 1980s focused narrowly on fuelwood supply and tree planting in South Asia and the Sahel, CFM has since evolved into a broad and diverse set of approaches — including participatory forest management, joint forest management, and community-based forest management — implemented across Asia, Africa, Latin America and Europe (Roberts and Gautam 2003; Baynes et al. 2015; Gilmour 2016).
The rationale for CFM rests on the premise that local communities, with vested interests in maintaining forest resource flows and access to place- and time-specific knowledge, can govern forests more effectively and sustainably than centralised state institutions (Hajjar et al. 2021). Where well-implemented, CFM has delivered substantive benefits: forest regeneration (by as much as 37% in Nepal), reduced deforestation, enhanced biodiversity conservation, improved rural livelihoods and incomes, and stronger local institutions and community cohesion (Gilmour 2016; Charnley and Poe 2007; Baynes et al. 2015). Community forestry also positions local communities to participate in emerging carbon, biodiversity and ecosystem service markets, offering new revenue streams for forest protection.
Nevertheless, the outcomes of CFM globally have been mixed (Roberts and Gautam 2003). Recurring challenges include weak institutional capacity, insecure land tenure, unclear forest boundaries and competing resource claims, inequitable benefit-sharing arrangements, limited social capital, governance capacity and business acumen among local communities, poor market access, and high certification costs for timber and non-timber forest products (NTFPs) (Wily 2004; McDermott and Schreckenberg 2009). The conditions most conducive to CFM success include strong government facilitation, a sound legal framework devolving management and user rights to communities, high social capital, sustained support from Non-government organisations (NGOs) or private partners, effective rule enforcement, and the development of viable forest enterprises (Pagdee, Kim, and Daugherty 2006; Blomley 2013; Baynes et al. 2015). Where one or more of these conditions are absent, communities may be formally enrolled in CFM programmes without gaining meaningful authority or tangible benefits.



In Zambia, the trajectory of CFM reflects a gradual transition across four generations of interventions (1980–2025), from early awareness campaigns and tree-planting initiatives to the large-scale adoption of formal community forest groups driven by REDD+ and carbon finance mechanisms (Moombe, Malesu, and Harrison 2025). The context for CFM in Zambia is shaped by a distinctive land tenure situation: approximately 94% of land is under customary ownership (Sichone 2008) and significant land-use decisions have historically resided with traditional chiefs. CFM in Zambia, therefore, involves devolution of authority not only from the state but also to some extent from traditional leadership structures, and has been driven as much by the need to transition to sustainable forest management (SFM) and enable communities to generate localised benefits, as by concerns over tenure security or management authority per se.
The legal and policy framework for CFM in Zambia is now well-established. The Forests Act No. 4 of 2015 and the Forests (Community Forest Management) Regulations (SI No. 11 of 2018) provide the principal legal basis for CFM, granting communities the right to apply for recognition as community forest management groups (CFMGs), prepare and implement management plans, and access forest user rights including rights to timber and carbon credits. However, Zambia is also losing forests at a rate of 250,000 to 300,000 hectares annually, alongside substantial carbon losses from forest degradation, making improved forest governance a national priority aligned with the country’s nationally determined contributions (NDCs) under the Paris Agreement.
Since the formal CFM framework came into effect, the sector has expanded rapidly. The carbon market in particular has accelerated the adoption of CFM in Zambia, offering a vehicle for partnerships between carbon trading companies, conservation NGOs, and local communities that benefit from forest protection (Moombe, Malesu, and Harrison 2025). As of March 2025, there were 351 CFMGs in Zambia, presiding over approximately 9.4 million hectares of community forests (CFs) across all ten provinces (Moombe, Malesu, and Harrison 2025). North-western Province has the largest area under CFM and Luapula the smallest. Despite this growth, implementation quality varies considerably. Documented challenges include inadequate exit strategies by supporting projects, inconsistent monitoring and follow-up support, low capacity in business and governance skills among CFMGs, insufficient funding to complete the formal establishment process, lack of clarity over benefit-sharing arrangements, poor representation of women despite their high dependence on NTFPs, and unresolved intra-community conflicts over land and resources (Moombe and Kaumba 2022; Lundu 2023).



With CFM expanding rapidly in Zambia, the Government of Zambia, donors, and other stakeholders have recognised a critical need to understand whether CFM is achieving its intended outcomes of improved SFM and sustainable development, and where improvements in CFM implementation are required. This study was designed to fill that knowledge gap. Specifically, the study aimed to: (i) assess whether CFM in Zambia has led to improved sustainable management of forests and other natural resources; (ii) understand whether CFM has contributed to sustainable development; (iii) identify opportunities for improved implementation of CFM, including through enhanced monitoring of outcomes; and (iv) generate policy recommendations for improved CFM implementation in Zambia.
To address these aims, we surveyed 75 CFMGs across eight provinces, combining focus group discussions (FGDs) with CFMG executives, female, male and youth community members, and individual interviews with key stakeholders, namely community members, traditional leaders, District Forestry Officers (DFOs), and NGO representatives. We examine CFM performance across four domains — governance, sustainable forest management, economic transformation, and equity and inclusion — and identify the factors associated with stronger or weaker outcomes across these domains.
The report is organised as follows. The Survey Overview chapter describes the sample of community forests and interviewees. It demonstrates that the sample is sufficiently representative to support national-level inference. The Governance chapter examines whether CFMGs have completed the required legal and administrative processes, established functional governance structures, and developed business plans. The Sustainable Forest Management chapter assesses the impacts of CFM on deforestation, charcoal production, fire frequency, wildlife, and natural resource management. The Economic Transformation chapter evaluates changes in access to forest resources, employment, income, and the realisation of carbon and other economic opportunities. The Equity and Inclusion chapter examines benefit sharing, gender equity, transparency, and the impacts of CFM on poverty. The Discussion chapter synthesises findings across all four domains and draws out the key messages for CFM policy and practice in Zambia. The Recommendations chapter translates these findings into concrete, actionable recommendations for government, NGOs, and carbon trading enterprises. Three appendices provide supporting material: Appendix I describes the methods in detail; Appendix II contains the full survey tool used in the FGDs and individual interviews; and Appendix III lists the 75 community forests surveyed.

title: Appendix I - Survey tool
text:
Community Forest Management in Zambia: Is It Working?

Focus Group Discussion (FGD) Tool for Assessing the Effectiveness of Community Forest Management (CFM)



Introduction
Hello. My name is …………………………………. and we are from the Center for International Forestry Research and International Center for Research in Agroforestry (CIFOR-ICRAF). We are conducting a study to assess the effectiveness of Community Forest Management (CFM). This study is part of the Sustainable Agriculture for Forestry Ecosystems (SAFE) project, co-funded by the European Union (EU) and the German Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development (BMZ). The project is being implemented by GIZ through the Ministry of Green Economy and Environment (MoGEE) as the main partner, in collaboration with the Ministry of Agriculture (MoA) as a key partner.
We would like to interview you for this study. The information and insights we collect from you are for research purposes only. It will otherwise remain confidential, and your names will not be explicitly or implicitly identified in the analysis produced by our team. We anticipate no risks to your participation in this interview/discussion.
Participation in this interview/discussion is voluntary, and you may refuse to participate, discontinue the interview/discussion at any time, or skip any question you do not want to answer. The discussion will take approximately 2 hours, and we encourage open and respectful sharing of experiences.

title: Sustainable Forest Management
text: Most respondents indicated that deforestation, charcoal production, and fire frequency were decreasing, wildlife populations were increasing, and natural resource management was improving within CFs. Most FDG groups also indicated that both deforestation and charcoal production were decreasing outside their CF, suggesting there is no leakage effect. Community female FDGs tended to have a more positive view of the impact of CFM compared to the other groups. However, it is important to note that a small number of FDG groups and individual interviewees suggested the situation was worsening in their CFs. It will be important to identify CFs that are doing less well and address the problems. Few CFMGs were practising natural forest restoration or establishing plantations. Hence, there is a considerable opportunity to expand the contribution of CFM to meeting national restoration and plantation targets.

title: Sustainable Forest Management

Most FDG groups also indicated that both deforestation and charcoal production were decreasing outside their CF, suggesting there is no leakage effect. Community female FDGs tended to have a more positive view of the impact of CFM compared to the other groups. However, it is important to note that a small number of FDG groups and individual interviewees suggested the situation was worsening in their CFs. It will be important to identify CFs that are doing less well and address the problems. Few CFMGs were practising natural forest restoration or establishing plantations. Hence, there is a considerable opportunity to expand the contribution of CFM to meeting national restoration and plantation targets.

title: Sustainable Forest Management
text:
Introduction
In this chapter, we consider the effect of community forestry on indicators of sustainable forest management. The information is based only on the responses of interviewees to our survey tool. A separate chapter will analyse satellite derived remote sensed data on fire frequency and deforestation.

Deforestation
We asked interviewees whether CFM has reduced deforestation inside their CF and whether or not deforestation has increased outside their CF. An increase in deforestation outside the community forest might represent leakage, whereby activities involving clearing forest that are proscribed in the CF are displaced to areas around the CF.

Deforestation inside the community forest


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    deforestation = case_match(
            impact_on_sfm_cfm_reduced_forest_clearing_cfma,
            "no_there_has_never_been_clearing_for_agriculture" ~ "No change",
            "Agricultural practices ceased way before CFM" ~ "No change",
            "One field for cassava" ~ "No change",
            "Yes there no more fields_  it's all forests" ~ "Significant",
            "Yes_ significant reduction. There was chitemene system before CFM" ~ "Significant",
            "no_increase" ~ "No change",
            "yes_moderate" ~ "Moderate",
            "yes_significant" ~ "Significant",
            .default = NA
    ),
    deforestation = factor(deforestation, levels = c("Significant","Moderate","No change")),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female", "Executive")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(deforestation) |>
  count(interview_type, deforestation) |>
  group_by(interview_type) |>
  mutate(
    prop = n / sum(n)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(y = interview_type, x = prop, fill = deforestation)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(prop >= 0.05, n, "")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    size = 3.5, fontface = "bold", colour = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = 0.01),
    name   = "Proportion of groups"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "No change"         = "#C0392B",
      "Moderate" = "#F0C040",
      "Significant"        = "#2A9D8F"
    ),
    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)
  ) +
  labs(
    y    = NULL,
    fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.title.x       = element_text(size = rel(1), colour = "grey40"),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    legend.text        = element_text(size = rel(1)),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )








Figure 1: ‘Has CFM reduced deforestation inside the community forest?’ The length of each bar segment represents the proportion of FGD groups in that category. The numbers superimposed on the bars represent the actual number of groups in each category.





A majority of groups report either a moderate or significant reduction in deforestation inside the CF. Moreover, these answers were consistent across the FGD groups. There were no significant differences in response.






NoteDeforestation - FGDs - Model methods





To examine the differences among FGD types in their assessment of whether CFM had reduced deforestation, we employed a cumulative link mixed model (CLMM) with random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We used FGD type as the factor. We examined the significance of pairwise differences among FGD types using Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    deforestation = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_cfm_reduced_forest_clearing_cfma,
      "no_there_has_never_been_clearing_for_agriculture" ~ "No change",
      "Agricultural practices ceased way before CFM"     ~ "No change",
      "One field for cassava"                            ~ "No change",
      "Yes there no more fields_  it's all forests"      ~ "Significant",
      "Yes_ significant reduction. There was chitemene system before CFM" ~ "Significant",
      "no_increase"    ~ "No change",
      "yes_moderate"   ~ "Moderate",
      "yes_significant" ~ "Significant",
      .default = NA
    ),
    deforestation = factor(deforestation,
                           levels = c("No change", "Moderate", "Significant"),
                           ordered = TRUE),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Executive", "Female", "Male", "Youth")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(deforestation) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M1 <- clmm(deforestation ~
              interview_type +
              (1 | province) +
              (1 | province_cfmg),
            data = test.data)

emm.M1     <- emmeans(M1, specs = ~ interview_type)
pairwise.M1 <- pairs(emm.M1, adjust = "Tukey")



Model results (click to expand)
Model results (click to expand)

Cumulative Link Mixed Model fitted with the Laplace approximation

No change|Moderate    -0.5714     0.4609  -1.240
Moderate|Significant   0.4295     0.4601   0.933

P value adjustment: tukey method for comparing a family of 4 estimates 




Among the individual interviewees (Table 1), again a majority indicated that CFM had reduced deforestation inside the CF. The differences among the interviewee roles were not significant.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    deforestation = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_cfm_reduced_forest_clearing_cfma,
      "no_there_has_never_been_clearing_for_agriculture" ~ "No change",
      "Agricultural practices ceased way before CFM"     ~ "No change",
      "One field for cassava"                            ~ "No change",
      "Yes there no more fields_  it's all forests"      ~ "Significant",
      "Yes_ significant reduction. There was chitemene system before CFM" ~ "Significant",
      "no_increase"    ~ "No change",
      "yes_moderate"   ~ "Moderate",
      "yes_significant" ~ "Significant",
      .default = NA
    ),
    deforestation = factor(deforestation,
                           levels = c("No change", "Moderate", "Significant")),
    interviewee_role = factor(
      case_match(
        interviewee_role,
        "cfmg_exec_member" ~ "CFMG executive member",
        "cfmg_member"      ~ "Community member",
        "trad_leader"      ~ "Traditional leader",
        "dfo"              ~ "District Forestry Officer",
        "NGO_rep"          ~ "NGO representative"
      ),
      levels = c("CFMG executive member", "Community member", "Traditional leader",
                 "District Forestry Officer", "NGO representative")
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(deforestation)) |>
  count(interviewee_role, deforestation, name = "obs") |>
  group_by(interviewee_role) |>
  mutate(n = sum(obs), prop = obs / n) |>
  ungroup() |>
  select(interviewee_role, n, deforestation, prop) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = deforestation, values_from = prop, values_fill = 0) |>
  arrange(interviewee_role) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(interviewee_role = "Role") |>
  fmt_percent(columns = c(`No change`, Moderate, Significant), decimals = 0) |>
  tab_header(
    title    = "Perceived reduction in deforestation inside the community forest by interviewee role",
    subtitle = "Values represent the % of respondents in each response category. n = the total number of interview responses."
  ) |>
  opt_align_table_header(align = "left") |>
  cols_align(align = "center", columns = -interviewee_role) |>
  cols_align(align = "left", columns = interviewee_role) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(align = "center"),
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = interviewee_role)
  ) |>
  sub_missing()




Table 1: Perceived reduction in deforestation inside the community forest by individual interviewee role.

NoteReduced forest clearing - Individual interviewees - Model methods





To examine differences among individual interviewees in their assessment of whether CFM had reduced deforestation, we employed a cumulative link mixed model (CLMM) with random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We included interviewee role, interviewee gender and interviewee age as factors. To examine pairwise differences among interviewee roles, we employed Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    deforestation = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_cfm_reduced_forest_clearing_cfma,
      "no_there_has_never_been_clearing_for_agriculture" ~ "No change",
      "Agricultural practices ceased way before CFM"     ~ "No change",
      "One field for cassava"                            ~ "No change",
      "Yes there no more fields_  it's all forests"      ~ "Significant",
      "Yes_ significant reduction. There was chitemene system before CFM" ~ "Significant",
      "no_increase"    ~ "No change",
      "yes_moderate"   ~ "Moderate",
      "yes_significant" ~ "Significant",
      .default = NA
    ),
    deforestation = factor(deforestation,
                           levels = c("No change", "Moderate", "Significant"),
                           ordered = TRUE),
    interviewee_gender = demo_information_interviewee_gender,
    interviewee_age    = demo_information_interviewee_age
  ) |>
  drop_na(deforestation) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M2 <- clmm(deforestation ~
              interviewee_role +
              interviewee_gender +
              interviewee_age +
              (1 | province) +
              (1 | province_cfmg),
            data = test.data)

emm.M2     <- emmeans(M2, specs = ~ interviewee_role)
pairwise.M2 <- pairs(emm.M2, adjust = "Tukey")

Deforestation outside the community forest
To investigate possible leakage effects, we also asked interviewees whether CFM had led to an increase in deforestation outside their community forest.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    deforestation = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_forest_clearing_increased_outside_cfma,
      "Clearing only done for field opening" ~ "No change",
      "In Mboshya there are no agricultural activities" ~ "No change",
      "It increases according to seasons_ especially during the tobacco harvesting" ~ "Moderate",
      "No change_ community members' concentration is on farming. They have appreciation for gover…" ~ "No change",
      "There has never been  cutting of trees out side the cfma" ~ "No change",
      "There has never been any forest clearing outside the cfma." ~ "No change",
      "There has never been cutting of trees" ~ "No change",
      "no_decrease" ~ "Decrease",
      "no_increase"    ~ "No change",
      "yes_moderate"   ~ "Moderate",
      "yes_significant" ~ "Significant",
      .default = NA
    ),
    deforestation = factor(deforestation,
                           levels = c("Significant", "Moderate", "No change", "Decrease")),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female", "Executive")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(deforestation) |>
  count(interview_type, deforestation) |>
  group_by(interview_type) |>
  mutate(
    prop = n / sum(n)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(y = interview_type, x = prop, fill = deforestation)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(prop >= 0.05, n, "")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    size = 3.5, fontface = "bold", colour = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = 0.01),
    name   = "Proportion of groups"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Decrease" = "#2A9D8F",
      "No change"   = "#76B7A0",
      "Moderate"    = "#F0C040",
      "Significant" = "#C0392B"
    ),
    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)
  ) +
  labs(
    y    = NULL,
    fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.title.x       = element_text(size = rel(1),    colour = "grey40"),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    legend.text        = element_text(size = rel(1)),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )








Figure 2: ‘Has CFM increased deforestation outside the community forest?’ The length of each bar segment represents the proportion of groups in that category. The numbers superimposed on the bars represent the actual number of groups in each category.





Contrary to the expectations of leakage, a majority of the FGD groups responded that CFM actually reduced deforestation outside their CF. Moreover, the responses were consistent across FGD groups. There were no significant differences in response.






NoteDeforestation outside the CF - FGDs - Model methods





To examine the differences among FGD types in their assessment of whether CFM had increased deforestation outside the CF, we employed a cumulative link mixed model (CLMM) with random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We used FGD type as the factor. We examined the significance of pairwise differences among FGD types using Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    deforestation = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_forest_clearing_increased_outside_cfma,
      "Clearing only done for field opening" ~ "No change",
      "In Mboshya there are no agricultural activities" ~ "No change",
      "It increases according to seasons_ especially during the tobacco harvesting" ~ "Moderate",
      "No change_ community members' concentration is on farming. They have appreciation for gover…" ~ "No change",
      "There has never been  cutting of trees out side the cfma" ~ "No change",
      "There has never been any forest clearing outside the cfma." ~ "No change",
      "There has never been cutting of trees" ~ "No change",
      "no_decrease" ~ "Decrease",
      "no_increase"    ~ "No change",
      "yes_moderate"   ~ "Moderate",
      "yes_significant" ~ "Significant",
      .default = NA
    ),
    deforestation = factor(deforestation,
                           levels = c("Significant", "Moderate", "No change", "Decrease")),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Executive", "Female", "Male", "Youth")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(deforestation) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M3 <- clmm(deforestation ~
              interview_type +
              (1 | province) +
              (1 | province_cfmg),
            data = test.data)

emm.M3     <- emmeans(M3, specs = ~ interview_type)
pairwise.M3 <- pairs(emm.M3, adjust = "Tukey")

Among the individual interviewees (Table 2), the individuals from the communities agreed that deforestation outside their CF had decreased. However, NGO representatives and DFOs were less likely to suggest deforestation had decreased outside the CF and more likely to suggest a moderate increase in deforestation.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    deforestation = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_forest_clearing_increased_outside_cfma,
      "Clearing only done for field opening" ~ "No change",
      "In Mboshya there are no agricultural activities" ~ "No change",
      "It increases according to seasons_ especially during the tobacco harvesting" ~ "Moderate",
      "No change_ community members' concentration is on farming. They have appreciation for gover…" ~ "No change",
      "There has never been  cutting of trees out side the cfma" ~ "No change",
      "There has never been any forest clearing outside the cfma." ~ "No change",
      "There has never been cutting of trees" ~ "No change",
      "no_decrease" ~ "Decrease",
      "no_increase"    ~ "No change",
      "yes_moderate"   ~ "Moderate",
      "yes_significant" ~ "Significant",
      .default = NA
    ),
    deforestation = factor(deforestation,
                           levels = c("Decrease", "No change", "Moderate", "Significant")),
    interviewee_role = factor(
      case_match(
        interviewee_role,
        "cfmg_exec_member" ~ "CFMG executive member",
        "cfmg_member"      ~ "Community member",

NoteDeforestation outside the CF - Individual interviewees - Model methods





To examine differences among individual interviewees in their assessment of whether CFM had increased deforestation outside the community forest, we employed a cumulative link mixed model (CLMM) with random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We included interviewee role, interviewee gender and interviewee age as factors. To examine pairwise differences among interviewee roles, we employed Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    deforestation = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_forest_clearing_increased_outside_cfma,
      "Clearing only done for field opening" ~ "No change",
      "In Mboshya there are no agricultural activities" ~ "No change",
      "It increases according to seasons_ especially during the tobacco harvesting" ~ "Moderate",
      "No change_ community members' concentration is on farming. They have appreciation for gover…" ~ "No change",
      "There has never been  cutting of trees out side the cfma" ~ "No change",
      "There has never been any forest clearing outside the cfma." ~ "No change",
      "There has never been cutting of trees" ~ "No change",
      "no_decrease" ~ "Decrease",
      "no_increase"    ~ "No change",
      "yes_moderate"   ~ "Moderate",
      "yes_significant" ~ "Significant",
      .default = NA
    ),
    deforestation = factor(deforestation,
                           levels = c("Significant", "Moderate", "No change", "Decrease")),
    interviewee_gender = demo_information_interviewee_gender,
    interviewee_age    = demo_information_interviewee_age
  ) |>
  drop_na(deforestation) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name))

M4 <- clmm(deforestation ~
              interviewee_role +
              interviewee_gender +
              interviewee_age +
              (1 | province) +
              (1 | province_cfmg),
            data = test.data)

emm.M4     <- emmeans(M4, specs = ~ interviewee_role)
pairwise.M4 <- pairs(emm.M4, adjust = "Tukey")


The differences in opinion between the CFMG executive members and NGO representatives and DFOs, and between traditional leaders and NGO representatives and DFOs, were significant.

Charcoal production
We asked interviewees whether CFM has reduced charcoal production inside their CF, and whether it has increased immediately outside their CF.

Charcoal production inside the community forest


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    charcoal = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_charcoal_production_in_cfma,
      "No. There have never been  charcoal  production  in this forest"    ~ "No change",
      "No_ charcoal production is not a common practice for community members"    ~ "No change",
      "Some areas are encroached by charcoal production"    ~ "No change",
      "There has never been  charcoal  production"    ~ "No change",
      "There has never been  charcoal  production in this forest"    ~ "No change",
      "yes_reduced"   ~ "Moderate",
      "yes_high" ~ "Significant",
      "no" ~ "Significant",
      "no_stopped" ~ "Significant",
      .default = NA
    ),
    charcoal = factor(charcoal,
                      levels = c("Significant", "Moderate", "No change")),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female", "Executive")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(charcoal) |>
  count(interview_type, charcoal) |>
  group_by(interview_type) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(y = interview_type, x = prop, fill = charcoal)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(prop >= 0.05, n, "")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    size = 3.5, fontface = "bold", colour = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = 0.01),
    name   = "Proportion of groups"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "No change"   = "#C0392B",
      "Moderate"    = "#F0C040",
      "Significant" = "#2A9D8F"
    ),
    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)
  ) +
  labs(y = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.title.x       = element_text(size = rel(1),    colour = "grey40"),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    legend.text        = element_text(size = rel(1)),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )








Figure 3: ‘Has CFM reduced charcoal production inside the community forest?’ The length of each bar segment represents the proportion of groups in that category. The numbers superimposed on the bars represent the actual number of groups in each category.





Almost all the FGD groups agreed that charcoal making inside their CF had decreased, with most indicating that charcoal making had decreased significantly. The small differences in responses among FGD groups were not significant.






NoteCharcoal production inside the CF - FGDs - Model methods





To examine the differences among FGD types in their assessment of whether CFM had reduced charcoal production inside the CF, we employed a cumulative link mixed model (CLMM) with random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We used FGD type as the factor. We examined the significance of pairwise differences among FGD types using Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    charcoal = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_charcoal_production_in_cfma,
      "No. There have never been  charcoal  production  in this forest"    ~ "No change",
      "No_ charcoal production is not a common practice for community members"    ~ "No change",
      "Some areas are encroached by charcoal production"    ~ "No change",
      "There has never been  charcoal  production"    ~ "No change",
      "There has never been  charcoal  production in this forest"    ~ "No change",
      "yes_reduced"   ~ "Moderate",
      "yes_high" ~ "Significant",
      "no" ~ "Significant",
      "no_stopped" ~ "Significant",
      .default = NA
    ),
    charcoal = factor(charcoal,
                      levels = c("No change", "Moderate", "Significant"),
                      ordered = TRUE),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Executive", "Female", "Male", "Youth")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(charcoal) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name)) |>
  droplevels()

if (nlevels(test.data$charcoal) >= 2) {
  M5 <- clmm(charcoal ~
                interview_type +
                (1 | province) +
                (1 | province_cfmg),
              data = test.data)
  emm.M5     <- emmeans(M5, specs = ~ interview_type)
  pairwise.M5 <- pairs(emm.M5, adjust = "Tukey")
} else {
  M5 <- NULL
  cat("Model not fitted: only", nlevels(test.data$charcoal),
      "level(s) present in charcoal after filtering.\n")
}

Charcoal production outside the community forest
We also asked interviewees whether CFM has led to an increase in charcoal production outside their CF.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    charcoal = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_charcoal_production_increased_outside_cfma,
      "no_reduced"    ~ "Decrease",
      "no_change"    ~ "No change",
      "yes_moderate_increase"   ~ "Moderate",
      "yes_significant_increase" ~ "Significant",
      "Yes_ moderate increase. Only for household use"   ~ "Moderate",
      "Moderate decrease"    ~ "Decrease",
      "Due to increase in population_ some charcoal production now being observed in some open are…"   ~ "Moderate",
      "CFMG members not involved in charcoal production"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal  production  is not allowed in the chiefdom"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal  production is not allowed in the chiefdom"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal production  is not allowed  in the chiefdom"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal production has been banned from the chiefdom"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal production is not common in this part of the district"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal production is not commonly practiced. There is no market for charcoal production"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal production is not practiced in the community"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal production not a common practice in the community"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal production only done when clearing an area for new agricultural fields"    ~ "No change",
      "No charcoal  production"    ~ "No change",
      "No charcoal  production In this area"    ~ "No change",
      "No charcoal  production in this community"    ~ "No change",
      "No charcoal production"    ~ "No change",
      "No charcoal production near CFMA"    ~ "No change",
      "No. There has never been charcoal  production"    ~ "No change",
      .default = NA
    ),
    charcoal = factor(charcoal,
                      levels = c("Significant", "Moderate", "No change", "Decrease")),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female", "Executive")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(charcoal) |>
  count(interview_type, charcoal) |>
  group_by(interview_type) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(y = interview_type, x = prop, fill = charcoal)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(prop >= 0.05, n, "")),

  )








Figure 4: ‘Has CFM increased charcoal production outside the community forest?’ The length of each bar segment represents the proportion of groups in that category. The numbers superimposed on the bars represent the actual number of groups in each category.





As can be seen, the vast majority of groups indicated that charcoal making had also decreased outside their CF. However, there were a small number that indicated charcoal making had increased, some indicating it had increased significantly. Female FGD groups were significantly more likely to indicate that charcoal making had decreased outside the CF than all other groups (CFMG executive members, male or youth community members). There were no significant differences among these other groups, who most often indicated that there was no change in the amount of charcoal making.






NoteCharcoal production outside the CF - FGDs - Model methods





To examine the differences among FGD types in their assessment of whether CFM had increased charcoal production outside the community forest, we employed a cumulative link mixed model (CLMM) with random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We used FGD type as the factor. We examined the significance of pairwise differences among FGD types using Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    charcoal = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_charcoal_production_increased_outside_cfma,
      "no_reduced"    ~ "Decrease",
      "no_change"    ~ "No change",
      "yes_moderate_increase"   ~ "Moderate",
      "yes_significant_increase" ~ "Significant",
      "Yes_ moderate increase. Only for household use"   ~ "Moderate",
      "Moderate decrease"    ~ "Decrease",
      "Due to increase in population_ some charcoal production now being observed in some open are…"   ~ "Moderate",
      "CFMG members not involved in charcoal production"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal  production  is not allowed in the chiefdom"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal  production is not allowed in the chiefdom"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal production  is not allowed  in the chiefdom"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal production has been banned from the chiefdom"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal production is not common in this part of the district"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal production is not commonly practiced. There is no market for charcoal production"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal production is not practiced in the community"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal production not a common practice in the community"    ~ "No change",
      "Charcoal production only done when clearing an area for new agricultural fields"    ~ "No change",
      "No charcoal  production"    ~ "No change",
      "No charcoal  production In this area"    ~ "No change",
      "No charcoal  production in this community"    ~ "No change",
      "No charcoal production"    ~ "No change",
      "No charcoal production near CFMA"    ~ "No change",
      "No. There has never been charcoal  production"    ~ "No change",
      .default = NA
    ),
    charcoal = factor(charcoal,
                      levels = c("Decrease", "No change", "Moderate", "Significant"),
                      ordered = TRUE),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Executive", "Female", "Male", "Youth")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(charcoal) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name)) |>
  droplevels()

if (nlevels(test.data$charcoal) >= 2) {
  M7 <- clmm(charcoal ~
                interview_type +
                (1 | province) +
                (1 | province_cfmg),
              data = test.data)
  emm.M7     <- emmeans(M7, specs = ~ interview_type)
  pairwise.M7 <- pairs(emm.M7, adjust = "Tukey")
} else {
  M7 <- NULL
  cat("Model not fitted: only", nlevels(test.data$charcoal),
      "level(s) present in charcoal after filtering.\n")
}

Fire
We asked interviewees about fire monitoring practices and whether CFM had reduced fire incidence inside their CF. We did not ask about changes in fire frequency outside the CF, because clearing fields for agriculture or improving grazing often involves burning.

Fire monitoring


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "cfmg_executive") |>
  mutate(
    response = substr(impact_on_sfm_fire_monitoring_in_cfma, 1, 1),
    response = case_match(
      response,
      "y" ~ "Yes",
      "n" ~ "No"
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(response)) |>
  count(province, response) |>
  mutate(
    n_signed = if_else(response == "No", -n, n),
    response  = factor(response, levels = c("No", "Yes"))
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(y = reorder(province, n_signed), x = n_signed, fill = response)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, colour = "white", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = n,
        hjust = if_else(response == "No", 1.4, -0.4)),
    size = 3.5, fontface = "bold", colour = "grey25"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = ~abs(.x),
    expand = expansion(mult = 0.18),
    name   = "Number of CFMGs"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("No" = "#E07B54", "Yes" = "#2A9D8F"),
    guide  = guide_legend(reverse = FALSE)
  ) +
  labs(
    y    = NULL,
    fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.title.x       = element_text(size = rel(1), colour = "grey40"),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    legend.text        = element_text(size = rel(1)),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )








Figure 5: Whether CFMGs have established fire monitoring in the community forest by province. The length of the bars represents the number of CFMGs either monitoring fires (Yes) or not (No). The numbers on the bars represent the actual number of CFMGs in each category.





As can be seen in the figure, most CFMGs have instigated a system of fire monitoring. However, ten CFMGs across four provinces from our sample of 75 did not have any system of fire monitoring.


Fire incidence inside the community forest
We asked interviewees whether CFM has reduced fire incidence inside their CF.


Code

  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(

    ),
    fire = factor(fire, levels = c("Significant", "Moderate", "No change","Increase")),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female", "Executive")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(fire) |>
  count(interview_type, fire) |>
  group_by(interview_type) |>
  mutate(
    prop = n / sum(n)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(y = interview_type, x = prop, fill = fire)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(prop >= 0.05, n, "")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    size = 3.5, fontface = "bold", colour = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = 0.01),
    name   = "Proportion of groups"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Increase" = "#C0392B",
      "No change"   = "#F0C040",
      "Moderate"    = "#2A9D8F",
      "Significant" = "#76B7A0"
    ),
    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)
  ) +
  labs(
    y    = NULL,
    fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.title.x       = element_text(size = rel(1), colour = "grey40"),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    legend.text        = element_text(size = rel(1)),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )








Figure 6: ‘Has CFM reduced fire incidence inside the community forest?’ The length of each bar segment represents the proportion of groups in that category. The numbers superimposed on the bars represent the actual number of groups in each category.





As can be seen, a majority of groups reported a moderate or significant reduction in fire incidence in the CF. Female FDGs reported more significant declines in fire frequency compared to the other groups, who reported more moderate declines. These differences between female FDG groups and the other FDG groups (CFMG Executives, male and youth) were significant.






NoteFire incidence - FGDs - Model methods





To examine the differences among FGD types in their assessment of whether CFM had reduced fire incidence, we employed a cumulative link mixed model (CLMM) with random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We used FGD type as the factor. We examined the significance of pairwise differences among FGD types using Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    fire = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_fire_incidence_reduction_cfma,
      "no_incidents_have_increased" ~ "Increase",
      "no_change"     ~ "No change",
      "yes_moderate_reduction"    ~ "Moderate",
      "yes_significant_reduction" ~ "Significant",
      "No change observed as at now" ~ "No change",
      .default = NA
    ),
    fire = factor(fire,
                  levels = c("Increase","No change", "Moderate", "Significant"),
                  ordered = TRUE),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Executive", "Female", "Male", "Youth")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(fire) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name)) |>
  droplevels()

if (nlevels(test.data$fire) >= 2) {
  M9 <- clmm(fire ~
                interview_type +
                (1 | province) +
                (1 | province_cfmg),
              data = test.data)
  emm.M9      <- emmeans(M9, specs = ~ interview_type)
  pairwise.M9 <- pairs(emm.M9, adjust = "Tukey")
} else {
  M9 <- NULL
  cat("Model not fitted: only", nlevels(test.data$fire),
      "level(s) present in fire after filtering.\n")
}

To examine differences among individual interviewees in their assessment of whether CFM had reduced fire incidence, we employed a cumulative link mixed model (CLMM) with random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We included interviewee role, interviewee gender and interviewee age as factors. To examine pairwise differences among interviewee roles, we employed Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>

      impact_on_sfm_fire_incidence_reduction_cfma,

      "yes_significant_reduction" ~ "Significant",
      "No change observed as at now" ~ "No change",
      .default = NA
    ),
    fire = factor(fire,
                  levels = c("Increase","No change", "Moderate", "Significant"),
                  ordered = TRUE),
    interviewee_gender = demo_information_interviewee_gender,
    interviewee_age    = demo_information_interviewee_age
  ) |>
  drop_na(fire) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name)) |>
  droplevels()

if (nlevels(test.data$fire) >= 2) {

                 interviewee_role +
                 interviewee_gender +

} else {
  M10 <- NULL
  cat("Model not fitted: only", nlevels(test.data$fire),
      "level(s) present in fire after filtering.\n")
}

      "level(s) present in wildlife after filtering.\n")
}

Forest resources management
We asked interviewees whether CFM had improved the management of forest resources inside their CF.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    frm = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_forest_resources_management,
      "no_management_has_worsened"    ~ "Management worse",
      "no_change"                     ~ "No change",
      "yes_moderate_improvement"      ~ "Moderate improvement",
      "yes_significant_improvement"   ~ "Significant improvement",
      .default = NA
    ),
    frm = factor(frm, levels = c("Significant improvement", "Moderate improvement",
                                 "No change", "Management worse")),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Youth", "Male", "Female", "Executive")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(frm) |>
  count(interview_type, frm) |>
  group_by(interview_type) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(y = interview_type, x = prop, fill = frm)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(prop >= 0.05, n, "")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    size = 3.5, fontface = "bold", colour = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = 0.01),
    name   = "Proportion of groups"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Significant improvement" = "#2A9D8F",
      "Moderate improvement"    = "#F0C040",
      "No change"               = "#E07B54",
      "Management worse"        = "#C0392B"
    ),
    guide = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE, nrow = 2)
  ) +
  labs(y = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),

    axis.text.x        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.title.x       = element_text(size = rel(1), colour = "grey40"),

  )








Figure 8: ‘Has CFM improved forest resources management inside the community forest?’ The length of each bar segment represents the proportion of groups in that category. The numbers superimposed on the bars represent the actual number of groups in each category.





As can be seen, while responses vary among FGD groups, most groups agreed that the management of forest resources had improved moderately or significantly under CFM, and only a small number of primarily youth groups considered that it had gotten worse. A very high proportion of community female FDG groups considered that the management of forest resources was significantly better under CFM, a difference that was statistically significant.






NoteForest resources management - FGDs - Model methods





To examine the differences among FGD types in their assessment of whether CFM had improved forest resources management, we employed a cumulative link mixed model (CLMM) with random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We used FGD type as the factor. We examined the significance of pairwise differences among FGD types using Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type != "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    frm = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_forest_resources_management,
      "no_management_has_worsened"    ~ "Management worse",
      "no_change"                     ~ "No change",
      "yes_moderate_improvement"      ~ "Moderate improvement",
      "yes_significant_improvement"   ~ "Significant improvement",
      .default = NA
    ),
    frm = factor(frm,
                 levels = c("Management worse", "No change",
                            "Moderate improvement", "Significant improvement"),
                 ordered = TRUE),
    interview_type = factor(
      case_match(
        interview_type,
        "cfmg_executive" ~ "Executive",
        "cfmg_female"    ~ "Female",
        "cfmg_male"      ~ "Male",
        "cfmg_youth"     ~ "Youth"
      ),
      levels = c("Executive", "Female", "Male", "Youth")
    )
  ) |>
  drop_na(frm) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name)) |>
  droplevels()

if (nlevels(test.data$frm) >= 2) {
  M13 <- clmm(frm ~
                interview_type +
                (1 | province) +
                (1 | province_cfmg),
              data = test.data)
  emm.M13      <- emmeans(M13, specs = ~ interview_type)
  pairwise.M13 <- pairs(emm.M13, adjust = "Tukey")
} else {
  M13 <- NULL
  cat("Model not fitted: only", nlevels(test.data$frm),
      "level(s) present in frm after filtering.\n")
}

Among the individual interviewees (Table 7) the vast majority of interviewees considered that the management of forest resources had improved under CFM. There was a significant difference in responses between community members and DFOs. The DFOs were more likely to suggest there had been no change or only a moderate improvement in forest resource management under CFM, compared to the much higher proportion of interviewees indicating a significant improvement among community representatives.


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    frm = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_forest_resources_management,
      "no_management_has_worsened"    ~ "Management worse",
      "no_change"                     ~ "No change",
      "yes_moderate_improvement"      ~ "Moderate improvement",
      "yes_significant_improvement"   ~ "Significant improvement",
      .default = NA
    ),
    frm = factor(frm,
                 levels = c("Management worse", "No change",
                            "Moderate improvement", "Significant improvement")),
    interviewee_role = factor(
      case_match(
        interviewee_role,
        "cfmg_exec_member" ~ "CFMG executive member",

        "trad_leader"      ~ "Traditional leader",

NoteForest resources management - Individual interviewees - Model methods





To examine differences among individual interviewees in their assessment of whether CFM had improved forest resources management, we employed a cumulative link mixed model (CLMM) with random intercepts for province and CF nested within province. We included interviewee role, interviewee gender and interviewee age as factors. To examine pairwise differences among interviewee roles, we employed Tukey HSD.





Code
test.data <- cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "individual_interview") |>
  mutate(
    frm = case_match(
      impact_on_sfm_forest_resources_management,
      "no_management_has_worsened"    ~ "Management worse",
      "no_change"                     ~ "No change",
      "yes_moderate_improvement"      ~ "Moderate improvement",
      "yes_significant_improvement"   ~ "Significant improvement",
      .default = NA
    ),
    frm = factor(frm,
                 levels = c("Management worse", "No change",
                            "Moderate improvement", "Significant improvement"),
                 ordered = TRUE),
    interviewee_gender = demo_information_interviewee_gender,
    interviewee_age    = demo_information_interviewee_age
  ) |>
  drop_na(frm) |>
  mutate(province_cfmg = interaction(province, cfmg_name)) |>
  droplevels()

if (nlevels(test.data$frm) >= 2) {
  M14 <- clmm(frm ~
                 interviewee_role +
                 interviewee_gender +
                 interviewee_age +
                 (1 | province) +
                 (1 | province_cfmg),
               data = test.data)
  emm.M14      <- emmeans(M14, specs = ~ interviewee_role)
  pairwise.M14 <- pairs(emm.M14, adjust = "Tukey")
} else {
  M14 <- NULL
  cat("Model not fitted: only", nlevels(test.data$frm),
      "level(s) present in frm after filtering.\n")
}

Restoration
We asked CFMG executives whether natural forest restoration is practiced inside the community forest. We restrict the responses for this question to CFMG Executives, because this is the groups that should be best informed about the activities of the CFMG (and the differences in responses among groups to this question are no of substantive interest).


Code
cfmg |>
  filter(interview_type == "cfmg_executive") |>
  mutate(
    response = substr(impact_on_sfm_natural_forest_restoration, 1, 1),
    response = case_match(
      response,
      "y" ~ "Yes",
      "n" ~ "No"
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(response)) |>
  count(province, response) |>
  mutate(
    n_signed = if_else(response == "No", -n, n),
    response  = factor(response, levels = c("No", "Yes"))
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(y = reorder(province, n_signed), x = n_signed, fill = response)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, colour = "white", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = n,
        hjust = if_else(response == "No", 1.4, -0.4)),
    size = 3.5, fontface = "bold", colour = "grey25"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = ~abs(.x),
    expand = expansion(mult = 0.18),
    name   = "Number of CFMGs"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("No" = "#E07B54", "Yes" = "#2A9D8F"),
    guide  = guide_legend(reverse = FALSE)
  ) +
  labs(
    y    = NULL,
    fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    axis.line.x.bottom = element_line(colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.5),
    axis.text.y        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.text.x        = element_text(size = rel(1.14), colour = "grey20"),
    axis.title.x       = element_text(size = rel(1), colour = "grey40"),
    legend.position    = "bottom",
    legend.text        = element_text(size = rel(1)),
    plot.margin        = margin(10, 25, 10, 10)
  )








Figure 10: Whether CFMGs undertake natural forest restoration in the community forest by province. The length of the bars represents the number of CFMGs either undertaking restoration (Yes) or not (No). The numbers on the bars represent the actual number of CFMGs in each category.





Natural forest restoration is being practiced across approximately half of the CFs in our sample, mostly in Muchinga, Central and Northern provinces. We also asked CFMG executives about the specific restoration methods applied inside their CF. The majority were conserving natural regeneration. Only one CFMG was involved in planting native species and removal of invasives was not practiced.
